entries.title: Horoscope for Thursday, March 12, 2026
entries.summary: <h2>Moon Alert</h2> <p>There are no restrictions to shopping or important decisions today. The moon is in Capricorn.</p><h2>Aries (March 21-April 19)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-290000" name="html-embed-module-290000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>so-so</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Tread carefully today because your interactions with bosses, parents, VIPs and the police might not go well. (An understatement?) Do not fuel the flame if you encounter conflict with someone. Use your best judgment and remember to never underestimate the power of courtesy. Zip your lip.</p><h2>Taurus (April 20-May 20)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> In discussions with others, avoid controversial subjects like politics, religion and racial issues today because they will discourage you. However, your dealings with friends, groups and organizations can be a positive experience if you choose your words carefully and steer clear of trouble.</p><h2>Gemini (May 21-June 20)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> When dealing with shared property, taxes, debt or the responsibility for someone or something, you might take the lead because you feel confident and energetic. In fact, you'll impress authority figures. However, discussions with friends and groups might be tricky.</p><h2>Cancer (June 21-July 22)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-290000" name="html-embed-module-290000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>so-so</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Steer clear of parents, bosses and authority figures to avoid disagreements today, especially because you might be excited about expressing your opinions on controversial issues. If you can't get everyone to agree, then you will have to agree to disagree.</p><h2>Leo (July 23-Aug. 22)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Even though you might be gung-ho about something at work today, or something perhaps related to your health or even a pet, expect opposition due to rules, regulations, or a different way of doing things in a different culture. Tread carefully and think before you speak or do anything.</p><h2>Virgo (Aug. 23-Sept. 22)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-290000" name="html-embed-module-290000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>so-so</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Romantic relationships are difficult today, which means you need to be patient, tolerant and thoughtful. Likewise, relations with your kids might also be challenging, perhaps because of financial issues or the cost of something. You might choose to listen to a partner or close friend — or not. It's your choice.</p><h2>Libra (Sept. 23-Oct. 22)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-290000" name="html-embed-module-290000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>so-so</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Today you have a lot of energy to work and get better organized; nevertheless, relations with partners and close friends will be challenging. You might be disappointed or discouraged by someone, or you might feel criticized or diminished. Either way, it's a win/lose today. Go gently.</p><h2>Scorpio (Oct. 23-Nov. 21)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-290000" name="html-embed-module-290000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>so-so</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> You might be enthusiastic in your communications with others today, only to find that people disagree with you, especially at work. You might also encounter obstacles dealing with issues that are pet-related. Be discerning. Figure out what works and avoid what doesn't.</p><h2>Sagittarius (Nov. 22-Dec. 21)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Romance, along with issues related to your kids, will be challenging for you today. Therefore, don't be demanding. Instead, work from home. You might make significant purchases for home or family. Steer clear of problems.</p><h2>Capricorn (Dec. 22-Jan. 19)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Today the moon is in your sign dancing with Mars, which gives you a take-charge energy. This is good. However, it is also at odds with Venus, Saturn and Neptune. This is not good. Look for where you can be effective and stick with those activities. Avoid hassles with home and family.</p><h2>Aquarius (Jan. 20-Feb. 18)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-290000" name="html-embed-module-290000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>so-so</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Today, you are best served by keeping a low profile. Relax. Work alone or behind the scenes. Don't make a big deal about anything. However, respect your moneymaking ideas or financial plans because they might pay off. (You're on the ball with money matters.)</p><h2>Pisces (Feb. 19-March 20)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-290000" name="html-embed-module-290000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>so-so</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> This is a tricky day because, in one way, a friend might invigorate you and encourage you to take over a project or run the show. However, at the same time, a friend or someone in a group might have financial disagreements with you. Step carefully today. In fact, take baby steps.</p><h2>If today is your birthday</h2><p>Actress Jaimie Alexander (1984) shares your birthday today. By nature, you're competitive. You're also intuitive, inquisitive and keen to explore new experiences. This is a year of learning and teaching. Take time to renew your spiritual or religious beliefs. Seek out philosophies that will give you a better understanding of the true meaning of your life.</p>
entries.author: Georgia Nicols

entries.title: Man killed, another injured in Austin shooting
entries.summary: <p>A man was killed and another injured in a shooting in Austin Wednesday evening, according to Chicago police.</p><p>Two men, 32 and 33, were inside an apartment in the 100 block of North Lotus Avenue at 7:43 p.m. when they were both shot by another person, police said in a statement. The 33-year-old was shot multiple times and taken to Mount Sinai Hospital where he died.</p><p>The 32-year-old was shot in the hand and taken to Stroger Hospital in good condition, police said.</p><p>No one was in custody, police said.</p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Man fatally shot in Auburn Gresham
entries.summary: <p>A man was fatally shot in Auburn Gresham Wednesday afternoon, according to Chicago police.</p><p>The man, 21, was in the 9000 block of South Eggleston Avenue at 4:28 p.m. when he was shot in the neck, police said in a statement. He was taken to Christ Medical Center where he later died.</p><p>No one is in custody, police said.</p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Feds dispute Skokie woman's claim she was held by ICE for 30 hours
entries.summary: <p>The Department of Homeland Security is disputing the story of Sundas “Sunny” Naqvi, a U.S. citizen from Skokie who <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/03/08/skokie-woman-citizen-sunny-naqvi-detained-customs-border-patrol-ohare-immigration-ice" target="_blank" ><u>claimed she and her colleagues were detained</u></a> for more than 30 hours by federal immigration authorities over the weekend.</p><p>The federal agency claimed she was released nearly an hour after she was flagged for secondary screening at O'Hare Airport Thursday. The agency posted photos on social media Wednesday evening purporting to prove that. But Kevin Morrison, a Cook County commissioner and Naqvi's friend since high school, told the Chicago Sun-Times the photos were doctored.</p><p>"Instead of releasing a clearly doctored photo, I call on DHS to release all of the relevant video at O’Hare Airport at the days and times in question," Morrison said in a statement Wednesday evening.</p><p>On Sunday, Morrison shared Naqvi's version of events at a news conference outside the ICE processing facility in suburban Broadview. He said Naqvi and five of her colleagues were detained at O’Hare by federal immigration agents for their “recent travel history” after returning from Istanbul.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" data-embed> <blockquote class="bluesky-embed" data-bluesky-uri="at://did:plc:ddphbgeot3wsgicyuvx3q67n/app.bsky.feed.post/3mgh3v4u7m22n" data-bluesky-cid="bafyreigrh2habaoourg5hrsbdcqbhkqhm6qqygyhgnb24tsiucu2ardpqe"><p lang="en">UPDATE: An attorney I am in contact with, asked for her cell phone number to share with someone with the US Attorney General‘s office to try to find the phone in the facility, conveniently enough shortly after her phone unlocks she sends out this text message 1/3</p>— <a href="https://bsky.app/profile/did:plc:ddphbgeot3wsgicyuvx3q67n?ref_src=embed">Kevin Morrison (@morrisonforil.bsky.social)</a> <a href="https://bsky.app/profile/did:plc:ddphbgeot3wsgicyuvx3q67n/post/3mgh3v4u7m22n?ref_src=embed">2026-03-07T05:30:03.234Z</a></blockquote><script async src="https://embed.bsky.app/static/embed.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div></div><p>Naqvi, 28, and the others were taken to the Broadview facility where they were then transferred to a facility in Dodge County, Wisconsin, sometime Friday evening, Morrison said. She was released around 5 a.m. Saturday after nearly 30 hours.</p><p>The Cook County Sheriff’s office said it searched the facility and found nothing. The Dodge County, Wisconsin, sheriff's office didn’t immediately respond to a request for comment.</p><p>Naqvi and her detained colleagues declined interviews with the Sun-Times at the news conference Sunday and declined to comment by phone Wednesday. Naqvi’s lawyer, Rob Held, also did not return calls.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/01/30/new-body-camera-footage-shows-aftermath-of-ice-officer-allegedly-attacking-activist-near-chicago" target="_blank" ><u></u></a><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2025/09/28/broadview-police-say-theyre-investigating-feds-over-attack-on-cbs-reporters-vehicle" target="_blank" ><u></u></a><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2025/11/13/lawyer-seeking-preliminary-injunction-in-lawsuit-against-broadview-over-protest-hours-appears-outside-ice-facility" target="_blank" ><u></u></a><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/01/30/new-body-camera-footage-shows-aftermath-of-ice-officer-allegedly-attacking-activist-near-chicago" target="_blank" ><u></u></a></p><p>The Department of Homeland Security first dismissed Naqvi's claims in a statement Monday, calling the story “blatantly false." The agency said she arrived at O’Hare Thursday at 10:21 a.m., was referred to secondary screening for three minutes, retrieved her bags and departed the airport at 11:43 a.m. The agency said the entire exchange was captured on video.</p><p>The agency said it contacted her on Friday to ask her to inform people she was OK and confirmed that the Cook County Sheriff’s office had contacted the Broadview ICE processing facility about her.</p><p>The federal agency also pointed to Naqvi's criminal record. Naqvi pleaded guilty in 2022 to making a false police report alleging sexual assault and served two years probation, according to count records. The case was then dismissed as part of a deferred prosecution agreement.</p><p>On Tuesday, the agency said, “Ms. Naqvi was not taken into custody or transferred to ICE for detention."</p><p>Late Wednesday, the Department of Homeland Security <a class="Link" href="https://x.com/DHSgov/status/2031879248068194404?s=20" target="_blank" ><u>posted screenshots</u></a> on social media purporting to show Naqvi exiting secondary screening.</p><p>Morrison said they contained a "clearly doctored photo" and called on the agency to release all of its video.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">HERE ARE THE RECEIPTS:<br><br>As we said Sunny Naqvi entered the CBP area at 10:21 am.<br><br>Surveillance footage from O’Hare CLEARLY shows her entering secondary inspection at 10:46 a.m., and leaving secondary to the public area at 11:42 a.m.<br><br>Her claims of spending 43 hours in DHS custody… <a href="https://t.co/GkqWBLS6sn">https://t.co/GkqWBLS6sn</a> <a href="https://t.co/SWOJmMulcy">pic.twitter.com/SWOJmMulcy</a></p>— Homeland Security (@DHSgov) <a href="https://twitter.com/DHSgov/status/2031879248068194404?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">March 11, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>Morrison isn't alone in questioning the federal agency’s credibility.</p><p>A federal judge found that former U.S. Border Patrol Cmdr. Gregory Bovino <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2025/11/06/judge-sara-ellis-operation-midway-blitz-tear-gas-deportation-campaign" target="_blank" ><u>lied in a court deposition</u></a> in November about his own use of tear gas in a confrontation with protesters in Little Village. And 17 of 32 defendants in federal cases related to Operation Midway Blitz <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2026/tracker-federal-prosecutions-chicago-status-trumps-immigration-blitz-ice" target="_blank" ><u>have been dismissed</u></a>, three of which came after grand juries <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2025/12/10/federal-grand-jury-rejects-another-midway-blitz-case-this-time-against-laugh-factory-manager?utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=12112025%20Morning%20Edition&utm_content=12112025%20Morning%20Edition+CID_249b96e5ffb43cc073e9614d6b1b88f8&utm_source=cst_campaign_monitor&utm_term=Case%20rejected&tpcc=cst_cm" target="_blank" ><u>refused to indict</u></a>.</p><p>Shortly after agents shot Silverio Villegas González to death last September in Franklin Park, DHS claimed an agent was “seriously injured” after allegedly being dragged by González's vehicle. But police body-camera video later showed the agent said he was <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2025/09/22/ice-officer-injuries-nothing-major-deadly-franklin-park-shooting-mexican-immigrant-chicago-video" target="_blank" ><u>“just shooken up a little</u></a>.”</p><p>Footage released in February also <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/02/10/marimar-martinez-the-chicago-woman-shot-by-border-patrol-agent-expected-to-file-lawsuit-release-evidence" target="_blank" ><u>contradicted the agency’s account</u></a> of when one of its agents shot Marimar Martinez in Brighton Park last fall. DHS later said it “stands by our press releases and statements. The facts of what happened did not change.”</p>
entries.author: Mohammad Samra

entries.title: Man charged with tossing liquid on Mexican dance studio students in Avondale
entries.summary: <p>A man faces misdemeanor charges after he allegedly threatened and threw liquid at children and their parents leaving a Avondale dance studio, according to Chicago police.</p><p>Robert Villanueve, 50, faces five counts of misdemeanor battery, police said in a statement.</p><p>Villanueve allegedly threw cups of liquid and threatened a number of children and adults around 10 p.m. Friday, police said.</p><p>A video of the incident posted to social media shows a man and woman, who wasn't charged, on a fire escape shouting, “You’re lucky ICE is not here.” The man, apparently Villanueve, then throws cups of liquid toward the group, according to the video. The group was leaving Ballet Folklorico, a traditional Mexican folk dance studio.</p><p>Chicago police said the victims were a boy, 13, three girls — ages 13, 14 and 15 — and three women — ages 43, 41 and 40. Police said there were other victims at the scene not listed in the report.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" border-radius: 4px; flex-grow: 0; height: 14px; width: 144px;"></div></div></a></div></blockquote> <script async src="//platform.instagram.com/en_US/embeds.js"></script> </div> </div></div><p>Ofelia Guerra, founder and executive director Bellt Folklorico, said the neighbors' accusations the dance school was throwing loud parties was "perpetuating harmful stereotypes about our community" and attempted to "justify aggressive behavior." </p><p>Guerra asked the community to respect the legal process as it was ongoing and said the group's focus remains on "protecting our students, supporting our families, and ensuring that our community understands the truth about who we are and the positive work we do.”</p><p><b></b></p><p></p><p><a class="Link" href="https://www.instagram.com/p/DVv4Aq4jjZS/" target="_blank" ><u></u></a></p><p>The dance studio also thanked Ald. Felix Cardona (31st), the Commission on Human Relations of the city of Chicago, the Mayor Brandon Johnson’s office and the CPD hate crime unit for connecting with the group to discuss the matter Tuesday.</p><p>Cardona announced Villanueve’s arrest on social media Wednesday, and urged people to allow the justice system to take its course, noting the privacy of the minor involved.</p><p>“While we understand the strong emotions surrounding this situation, we ask that community members refrain from attacking or harassing the assailant,” Cardona said in a statement.</p><p>Villanueve is set to appear in court April 9.</p>
entries.author: Violet Miller

entries.title: Northwestern bounces Indiana 74-61 in Big Ten tournament second-round game to earn another shot at Purdue
entries.summary: <p>Chris Collins took Nick Martinelli out of the game with 8.9 seconds left, snarled at him, dapped him up, grabbed him by the scruff of his neck and yanked him toward a hard-earned place on a very happy <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/college-sports" target="_blank" >Northwestern</a> bench.</p><p>One of those moments between a coach and a star player that says it all, even if no words were spoken.</p><p>Get a load of the Wildcats, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/college-sports/2026/03/10/big-ten-tournament-opening-night-northwestern-penn-state-indiana-chris-collins-nick-martinelli" target="_blank" >the Big Ten tournament’s No. 15 seed</a>, who advanced to the third round by beating No. 10 seed Indiana 74-61 at the United Center. Get a load of Martinelli, owner of the school’s single-season scoring record — 734 points and counting — who dropped a 28-bomb on the Hoosiers. Get a load of Collins, who was fired up after the game, too, referencing a Sun-Times back-page headline that took full note of the Wildcats’ loss-filled season.</p><p>“We’ve played so much better than our (15-18) record, even though I woke up today and the headline in the paper said it was a program going nowhere,” Collins said. “I don’t know about that.”</p><p>Where the Wildcats are going is into a 5:30 p.m. Thursday matchup against No. 7 seed Purdue, always one of the conference’s heavy hitters. But Collins’ teams have had some recent success against the Boilermakers, and the Wildcats came close to what would have been a major upset last week in Evanston, losing 70-66.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/college-sports/2026/03/10/big-ten-tournament-opening-night-northwestern-penn-state-indiana-chris-collins-nick-martinelli" >On dud of a Big Ten Tournament opening night, at least Northwestern won a game</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/college-sports/2026/03/08/big-ten-tournament-illinois-michigan-nebraska-purdue-united-center-chicago" target="_blank" >Big Ten tournament preview: An Illinois-Michigan rematch Saturday at the United Center would be huge</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>It might still be an impossible road to the NCAA Tournament for Northwestern, which would have to win six games in six days to land an automatic bid as conference tournament champion. As it is, two wins — including the first-rounder against Penn State — equals Northwestern’s total over the previous eight years.</p><p>But at least the Wildcats could puff out their chests while the Hoosiers (18-14) were left to ponder the very real possibility of missing the Big Dance themselves. Like Northwestern, Indiana has never won the Big Ten tournament, which is in its 29th year. Who would’ve believed that could happen way back when this event was created?</p><p>And who would have believed Northwestern would ever beat Indiana seven times in a row? That streak might even grow next season. But first comes another shot to keep this season alive.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">Earned another one fellas 🪨⛏️🟣 <a href="https://t.co/ju7knBLyyP">pic.twitter.com/ju7knBLyyP</a></p>— Northwestern Basketball (@NUMensBball) <a href="https://twitter.com/NUMensBball/status/2031902940886081927?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">March 12, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p></p>
entries.author: Steve Greenberg

entries.title: Couple killed after storms bring at least 4 twisters, possible record-setting hail in Illinois and Indiana
entries.summary: <p>Nick Cronin rummaged through the remnants of his mother’s home, where she was found in a bathtub hours earlier after a tornado ripped through a small neighborhood in Kankakee.</p><p>Cronin picked up a license plate and tossed it back down as he stepped through the rubble and surveyed the destruction around him.</p><p>“I know everybody wants to come see the damage, but these are real people,” Cronin said through tears Wednesday, gesturing to a line of drivers gawking at the leveled homes.</p><p>His mother, in her 50s, was rescued Tuesday night from her bathtub that sat a day later uncovered and surrounded by debris from her home. She remains in an intensive care unit, said Cronin, who briefly stopped at his mother’s home to salvage any belongings.</p><p>One supercell spawned at least four tornadoes across Livingston and Kankakee counties in Illinois, and Newton, Jasper, and Starke counties in Northwest Indiana, the National Weather Service said.</p><p>The tornadoes damaged homes and buildings from Kankakee to about 25 miles east in Lake Village, Indiana — and beyond.</p><p>A couple from Lake Village died in the storm that hit just before 7 p.m. Tuesday. Officials identified them as Edward L Kozlowski, 89, and his wife Arlene Kozlowski, 84. </p><p>Several injuries were also reported from the severe storms that produced potentially record-setting hail in Illinois and Indiana.</p><p>"It hit us hard," Lori Postma, a spokesperson for Indiana's Lake Township Fire Department, said at a news conference Wednesday. "We will be working feverishly to bring our little town back to where it once was."</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item"> <bsp-carousel class="Carousel aria-label="Next"> <svg class="FlickityCustomNavigation-button-icon"><use xlink:href="#icon-right"></use></svg> </button> </div> </div></div> </bsp-carousel> </div> </div><p></p><h3><b>‘It’s a really nice neighborhood, when it was a neighborhood’</b></h3><p>Bob Wehrle, 60, saw debris flying across the empty field in his Kankakee backyard and rushed to his basement. Minutes later, he was buried underneath his collapsed tri-level home.</p><p>He was trapped for 30 minutes until he heard someone yell his name.</p><p>“I didn’t know who it was but I let them know. I actually had a broom down there and I was holding the broom up, waving the broom so they could see where I was,” Wehrle said.</p><p>The neighbor dug Wehrle out and soon after. Wehrle’s brother and nephew pulled him from the basement.</p><p>Wehrle’s wife had gone to work at Riverside Hospital a few hours before the tornado struck. Both were uninjured, and were left with nothing but some tools and a safe. His Dodge pickup truck was wrecked and in a ditch. A trailer was wrapped around a tree in his front yard.</p><p>“This is where we’ve been for 30 years, me and my wife. We don’t have kids, so this was it,” Wehrle said. “It’s a really nice neighborhood, when it was a neighborhood. It was nice and quiet out here.”</p><p>Wehrle’s and Cronin’s mother’s homes were among numerous destroyed houses on Sandbar Road, along the Kankakee River.</p><p>Hours after surviving the tornado in the middle of their living room, Jacob Cantrell, 37, noticed one neighbor — Cronin’s mother — still hadn’t responded to his text.</p><p>He tracked her down through a Facebook friend of hers and learned she was in the ICU. It all brought to reality how “blessed and grateful” he and his wife felt, he said. Two trees in their yard were uprooted, narrowly missing their home.</p><p>“It wasn’t until [Wednesday] morning, when I walked out to see my neighbors’ homes, that I understood how close we were to having our home destroyed and possibly ourselves,” said Ashley Cantrell, 37.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Ashley and Jacob Cantrell stand in the doorway of their home Wednesday on South Sandbar Road near East 3rd Street in Kankakee after tornadoes moved through the area the night before. Multiple trees fell on the Cantrell’s property, but their home was not destroyed in the storm.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Ashlee Rezin/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Wehrle said he does not know what he will do next. He’s staying with family nearby for now. He is pondering whether to rebuild his home at the same site, which is susceptible to flooding from the Kankakee River.</p><p>The flooding has impacted the same neighborhood in years past, said the Cantrells, who have two “escape kayaks” in their yard.</p><p>“This is not our first disaster that we’ve had to deal with,” Ashley Cantrell said. Jacob Cantrell spent Tuesday night and Wednesday morning helping neighbors board up their homes, while friends and neighbors came to check on them.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" data-embed> <iframe src="https://flo.uri.sh/visualisation/28010225/embed" frameborder="0" scrolling="no" height="575" width="700" style="width:100%;" title="Interactive or visual content"></iframe> </div> </div></div><p></p><h3><b>‘I didn’t think he was going to survive’</b></h3><p>In Lake Village, Indiana, Steve Travis knew a tornado was close and thought that could be it for him.</p><p>“I was looking out the front door. I saw a dumpster get picked up and moved," the 67-year-old said Wednesday, recalling his turbulent Tuesday. "I ran in toward my bedroom. When I got to the bedroom, the ceiling fell in."</p><p>Travis said he knew the storm was going to be bad since the winds were speeding at 80 to 90 mph.</p><p>“I knew it was time to hide,” he said. "I lost everything.”</p><p>Travis’s daughter Amber Hacker lives in Lowell, Ind., about 15 minutes north of her father’s house. She thought the tornado would be passing through there, so she and her family were sheltering in their crawlspace.</p><p>Once she could get there, and saw the condition of the building, "I didn’t think he was going to survive," Hacker said. “The roof is gone, the windows, the door. The ceiling and insulation had come down on him. ... I had to wait for an hour until they got him out."</p><p>The Lake Township, Ind. Fire Department, along with several other agencies, were out until about 4 a.m. Wednesday conducting primary searches of all damaged areas, officials said.</p><div class="Enhancement" through Lake Village, Indiana.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Amber Hacker photo</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>About 12,000 Northern Indiana Public Service Co. customers were impacted by the storms. </p><p>In Kankakee, hail between three to five inches in diameter fell. One six-inch hailstone possibly set an Illinois state record, according to the weather service. A hailstone measured at 4.8 inches in diameter was reported in Darien, Illinois.</p><p>Another supercell produced hailstones two to four inches in diameter across the southern and western Chicago metro area, officials said.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-470000" 2x" width="490" height="275" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Wreckage of a home in the village of Aroma Park, southeast of Kankakee, on Wednesday after tornadoes moved through the area the night before.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>It all capped a rollercoaster of weather in the Chicago area. Monday's high temperature of 73 degrees at O'Hare Airport set a record for the date, but was followed by chilly, cloudy weather Tuesday — until temperatures increased again as tornadoes moved in.</p><p>By midday Wednesday, the tornadoes had moved on and near-freezing temperatures were back. Snow was reported in Glenview and Lake View, while sleet mixed with a little snow had been reported in Oak Park. And a few hours later, the sun was breaking through the clouds again.</p><p>While weather service officials are still assessing the severity of Tuesday night's storms, the tornadoes appear to be more severe than the powerful derecho storms that brought 32 total tornadoes to the Chicago area on July 14-15, 2024.</p><p></p><p>The two "dominant" supercell thunderstorms that resulted in the tornadoes is a rotational storm that lasts longer, making it more favorable for large tornadoes and massive hail.</p><p>"We don't see large supercell thunderstorms in our area too often. You see that [in] Oklahoma and Texas a lot," said Kevin Donofrio, a meteorologist with the National Weather Service's Romeoville office. </p><p>Donofrio noted that while it's not uncommon for parts of northern Illinois and Northwest Indiana to see tornado weather as warmer weather approaches, the severity of Tuesday's storms was a rare sight.</p><p>When Lake Michigan is extremely cold in the spring, warm weather episodes create a frontal boundary that sets up right along the Interstate 80 corridor.</p><p>"South of that I-80 corridor tends to be an area that would be more favored for tornadoes," Donofrio said. "We don’t often see the magnitude of these types of tornadoes."</p><p>The next chance for thunderstorms is Saturday, though the severity level for those storms is not yet known.</p><p></p><p></p><p></p><p>The weather service was sending survey teams to assess the storm damage Wednesday.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" entries.author: Mohammad Samra

entries.title: White Sox shortstop Colson Montgomery is a hot shot but not a hot hitter
entries.summary: <p>GLENDALE, Ariz. — Infielder Colson Montgomery won the three-point shooting contest among his fellow <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" target="_blank" >White Sox</a> teammates outside the team’s training facility Wednesday, which shouldn’t surprise anyone.</p><p>Growing up in small-town Indiana — Holland, population 619 — Montgomery was as proficient in basketball as he now is in baseball. Hoosiers basketball, of course, runs in the blood.</p><p>Montgomery defeated Mike Vasil, Ryan Borucki and Zach Franklin by making 12 of 20 shots. That’s 60%, much better than the average from three-point range in the NBA right now of between 36% and 37%.</p><p>By contrast, he’s batting .185 this spring after hitting .239 in 71 games last season after being called up from the minors July 4.</p><p>Despite all that, manager Will Venable said before the Sox played the Angels at Camelback Ranch that Montgomery will be on the Opening Day roster and will be at shortstop March 26 in Milwaukee. He’ll bounce between short and third base.</p><p>‘‘He’s been having a good spring,’’ Venable said of Montgomery. ‘‘The pregame work has been great. There have been some barrels there recently. He’s still coming along, trying to find his way at the plate. But the defensive stuff, the running stuff we’ve asked him to do, he’s been outstanding.’’</p><p>It was a dream of Montgomery’s as a kid to be out there on Opening Day, and this will be his first one in the majors.</p><p>‘‘I think it’s a dream of everyone,’’ he said.</p><p>Baseball can be a killer with its large doses of failure.</p><p>As Bulls legend Michael Jordan discovered in his aborted transition from basketball to baseball, hitting a ball is perhaps the hardest thing to do in sports. Jordan’s career shooting percentage in the NBA was 49.7%. His batting average in 1994 during one season playing for Birmingham was .202. And that was at Double-A.</p><p>Montgomery, 24, is like many of the young Sox players: a work in progress. He’s 5-for-27 with two home runs, three RBI and eight strikeouts this spring.</p><p>It’s the dog days of camp, for sure.</p><p>‘‘When you get to this time of the spring, the days seem the same and start getting a little stagnant,’’ Montgomery said after smacking a hit in four at-bats Tuesday against the A’s in Mesa. ‘‘You just have to keep being process-oriented. Just come in and do the work you need to do to play 162.’’</p><p>Montgomery had to make the choice between basketball and baseball early in his amateur career. He was recruited by Purdue and Louisville to play basketball, but he chose baseball out of high school when the Sox selected him with the 22nd pick in the 2021 draft. At 6-3, he’s the all-time leading scorer in Southridge High School basketball history with 1,966 points.</p><p>The Sox, however, gave him a $3 million signing bonus, which made his decision pretty easy.</p><p>Montgomery now is playing for Venable, who had to make a similar decision between baseball and basketball, albeit at Princeton University, where he excelled at both sports.</p><p>‘‘I always liked basketball better until I made baseball my profession,’’ Venable said.</p><p>And for good reason. Venable averaged 10 points for Princeton in basketball and hit .344 in his first season playing baseball, earning All-Ivy League honorable mention.</p><p>‘‘I was always a basketball guy but learned later in high school and early in college that playing it after college was really not my path,’’ Venable said.</p><p>Montgomery made a similar decision, and now he’s following his own path.</p><p>‘‘My goal every day is just to get everything out of each day,’’ he said. ‘‘Just show up and try to be the best person [and] the best player I can every single day. If I do that, everything is going to fall in place. It’s a building block every day. That’s the way I look at it. Baseball is such a tough game.’’</p><p>On Wednesday, he fell back on his other sport. Swish.</p><div class="PageListL" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" data-show-loadmore="true" ><a class="AnchorLink" id="list-4a0005" entries.title: Lizzo, Labrinth, Ricky Martin and Hugh Jackman to make their Ravinia debuts in packed summer lineup
entries.summary: <p>Labrinth, Lizzo, Ricky Martin, Hugh Jackman and Chicago’s own Chance the Rapper will debut at Ravinia this summer, part of a historic season that features the reopening of the venue’s renovated iconic pavilion.</p><p>The freshly named and redesigned Hunter Pavilion is part of a $75 million multi-year renovation of Ravinia’s 36-acre music park in north suburban Highland Park. Last year, the festival revealed the first of its renovated venues, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/music/2025/08/09/ravinia-renovations-the-audrey-grand-reopening-2026" target="_blank" ><u>The Audrey</u></a>.</p><p>Lizzo, Labrinth and the rock guitarist St. Vincent will each perform with orchestras. The first concert at the Hunter Pavilion takes place July 11, when 21-year-old South Korean pianist Yunchan Lim joins the CSO alongside conductor Marin Alsop with Maurice Ravel’s “Piano Concerto in G major.” Lizzo will also perform the flute with the CSO that night, too.</p><p>“Ravinia does represent a lot of genres, but most importantly, it represents the importance of music education,” Ravinia CEO Jeff Haydon said. “Lizzo was inspired to pursue her music career because of teachers that she had in elementary school, and later on, to keep pursuing her love of the flute.”</p><p>St. Vincent will collaborate with Chicago Symphony Orchestra’s Jules Buckley to deliver deep cuts from across her catalog on July 25. <a class="Link" href="https://music.apple.com/us/artist/labrinth/205732582" target="_blank" ><u>Labrinth</u></a>, the British singer-songwriter and composer known for his work on the score of season 1 of HBO’s “Euphoria,” makes his Ravinia debut with the Chicago Philharmonic on Sept. 2.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-850000" name="image-850000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>St. Vincent, pictured here at Riot Fest in Douglass Park in 2024, will perform at Ravinia this summer with the Chicago Symphony Orchestra. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Ashlee Rezin/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Dozens of legendary performers will return to perform throughout the season, including Paul Simon (July 17-18), Gladys Knight (Aug. 11), Bonnie Raitt (Aug. 22), Brandi Carlile (Aug. 26) and Jacob Collier (Sept. 23).</p><p>The season opens on June 3 with Terence Blanchard and Ravi Coltrane bringing a night of jazz to the Martin Theater.</p><p>Ravinia’s upcoming season celebrates the 90th anniversary of the Chicago Symphony Orchestra’s residency, and it will feature a rare appearance by future music director <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/classical-music/2026/02/26/klaus-makela-conductor-cso-chicago-art-institute-tour" target="_blank" ><u>Klaus Mäkelä,</u></a> who personally asked Ravinia leadership if he could stop by to conduct the orchestra this summer.</p><p>This year’s six-week CSO residency will last through Aug. 16. Mäkelä will appear at the pavilion on Aug. 6 and 8, first for Jean Sibelius’s dark and beautiful “Violin Concerto” with Swedish soloist Daniel Lozakovich. Then the young conductor will channel Parisian ballet for Igor Stravinsky’s complete score from “The Firebird.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-710001" name="image-710001"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/905882e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F69%2Fa1%2F876a8f7246e0b392647b8a9f5aa8%2Fmakela-040524-8.jpeg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f71b17d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F69%2Fa1%2F876a8f7246e0b392647b8a9f5aa8%2Fmakela-040524-8.jpeg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f7c787c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F69%2Fa1%2F876a8f7246e0b392647b8a9f5aa8%2Fmakela-040524-8.jpeg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="MAKELA-040524-8.jpg. Klaus Mäkelä conducts the Chicago Symphony Orchestra through Saul Zinovjev’s Batteria’ at the Symphony Center's Orchestra Hall on Thursday night. It was the maestro's first tur at the podium since being named music director of the CSO." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f7c787c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F69%2Fa1%2F876a8f7246e0b392647b8a9f5aa8%2Fmakela-040524-8.jpeg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5edd12f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F69%2Fa1%2F876a8f7246e0b392647b8a9f5aa8%2Fmakela-040524-8.jpeg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f7c787c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F69%2Fa1%2F876a8f7246e0b392647b8a9f5aa8%2Fmakela-040524-8.jpeg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Klaus Mäkelä conducts the Chicago Symphony Orchestra through Saul Zinovjev’s Batteria’ at the Symphony Center’s Orchestra Hall in 2024. The music director designate for the CSO plans to appear with the orchestra at Ravinia in 2026. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>As for contemporary music, Chicago-born Quincy Jones will get a tribute concert on Aug. 13, when one of his longtime collaborators, Jules Buckley, will lead the CSO in a catalogue of his greatest hits. </p><p>Actor and singer Hugh Jackman will perform with the Chicago Philharmonic on Aug. 9, and Ricky Martin will make his Ravinia debut on Aug. 20.</p><p>Haydon said the renovations on site were done with “music in mind.”</p><p>The Hunter Pavilion will feature roomier chairs and a bigger stage. There are fewer seats than before, but the layout makes the space a lot more intimate, with seats feeling "practically on top of the stage," Haydon said. The pavilion also got a new sound system and an updated backstage area last year.</p><p>Tickets go on sale at <a class="Link" href="http://ravinia.org" target="_blank" ><u>Ravinia.org</u></a> on April 23, but donors can request them as early as March 17.</p><p>Here are some other season highlights:</p><ul class="rte2-style-ul" style="margin-top:0;margin-bottom:0;padding-inline-start:48px;" id="rte-bcd7c933-1d7a-11f1-9702-95cdcdb2a56d"><li>Billy Idol will debut July 12. </li><li>On July 16 and 18, James Conlon will lead Mozart’s “The Abduction from the Seraglio.”</li><li>On July 31, Marin Alsop will conduct the CSO in Dvořák’s famous Ninth Symphony, which borrows themes of Indigenous and Black American music. The evening will also feature Carlos Simon’s “Good News Mass,” a Ravinia co-commission infused with gospel. </li><li>On Aug. 14, polymathic conductor and producer Steve Hackman presents his take on Brahms’ First Symphony.</li><li>On Aug. 21, Alabama Shakes will appear with Chicago musician Liam Kazar. </li><li>On Aug. 27, Kool & The Gang performs. </li><li>Powerhouse baritone Tom Jones, with a career spanning five decades, returns to Ravinia on Sept. 18.</li><li>Family programming includes the Black Moon Trio’s “The Great Lakes” concert at the Sandra K. Crown Theater on Aug. 8 to JAM Orchestra’s “Dr. Seuss Goes to the Opera” in the Martin on Aug. 29.</li></ul>
entries.author: Ambar Colón

entries.title: Dear Abby: Wife plays second fiddle to husband's reputation
entries.summary: <p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> My husband, "Al," and I are retired and live on a farm with a great son and daughter-in-law. Al is the best person I have ever known, and he adores me. Everyone loves and respects him, and they seek his friendship, approval and advice, which is usually right. He's a rarity. Our son "Trent" and his wife idolize him.</p><p>My issue? When people, especially the kids, need advice or have questions, they call Al. If they need help, they call me and ask for Dad. (I am never asked or called.) The kids do love me, but Al has the "magic." I'm terrified at the idea of him passing before me. I'm afraid everyone will be mad at me for not dying first. This is not self-pity. I'd just like to feel needed and wise, too. Please advise me on how to not feel worthless because I'm not like my husband. — <i>OVERLOOKED IN SOUTH CAROLINA</i></p><p><b>DEAR OVERLOOKED:</b> In your marriage, your husband is the dominant partner, the figure who gets the majority of the attention. This happens often with politicians and entertainers. It takes a special kind of spouse to be as supportive as you are and have been.</p><p>I'm sorry you don't value yourself more highly. You have successfully raised a son and have a healthy relationship with your daughter-in-law. Because you feel so deep in Al's shadow, it's time to talk to him and your son about this. They may not realize how sad you feel.</p><p>As to your fear about him "dying first," women usually outlive their husbands. No one would "hate" you for surviving. You are neither worthless nor a nonentity; you are simply someone who is unaware of her own value.</p><p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> Some of my very good friends are married to less-than-stellar partners. There are money issues, infidelity, abuse and everything in between. I hear about their issues constantly, and I don't know how to respond anymore. It's difficult seeing close friends struggle when there's not much I can do to help.</p><p>I know these friends are not asking for help — just venting — but thanks to phones and texting, I get updated on their drama every day. I have muted their texts and respond less often. Despite all of this, I still find it very difficult knowing the struggle they are experiencing. It makes my chest hurt and my heart heavy. How do I navigate this and protect my sanity? — <i>LISTENER IN TEXAS</i></p><p><b>DEAR LISTENER:</b> Navigate this and protect your sanity by being honest with these friends. Tell them you care very much that they are having marital troubles, but you are not qualified to help solve their issues. (It's the truth.) Then advise them to consult a marriage counselor, psychologist or an organization that supports victims to help them break the cycle. If you do, it may lessen your stress level as well as theirs.</p><p><i>Dear Abby is written by Abigail Van Buren, also known as Jeanne Phillips, and was founded by her mother, Pauline Phillips. Contact Dear Abby at www.DearAbby.com or P.O. Box 69440, Los Angeles, CA 90069.</i></p><p>What teens need to know about sex, drugs, AIDS and getting along with peers and parents is in "What Every Teen Should Know." Send your name and mailing address, plus check or money order for $8 (U.S. funds) to: Dear Abby, Teen Booklet, P.O. Box 446, Kings Mills, OH 45034-0446. (Shipping and handling are included in the price.)</p>
entries.author: Abigail Van Buren

entries.title: No logic or clear answers to Trump's Iran war
entries.summary: <p>If you ask President Donald Trump, he’ll tell you we’ve already won the war in Iran.</p><p>When asked for an <a class="Link" href="https://www.axios.com/2026/03/11/trump-iran-war-end-withdrawal" target="_blank" >update by Axios</a> on Wednesday, Trump responded with the kind of upbeat nonchalance and flippant boastfulness you’d usually see when asked about the progress on one of his hotels.</p><p>"The war is going great," he said. "We are way ahead of the timetable. We have done more damage than we thought possible, even in the original six-week period." He then offered that there’s "practically nothing left to target."</p><p>As for an ending? "Any time I want it to end, it will end."</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-e40000" name="module-e40000"></a> <div class="RichTextSidebarModule-title">Columnist </div> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody">Columnist</div> </div><p>How, exactly, is it "going great"? What is the "timetable"? Shouldn’t it end when the mission is achieved and not when Trump simply wants it to?</p><p>His administration has simultaneously given no rationale to justify our strikes on Iran — failing to prove we were the target of an imminent attack — and all the reasons we had to, from regime change, to oil, to support for Israel.</p><p>It’s sent mixed messages on timing, promising both that it’s practically over and that it could take awhile. And it’s been unreliable in its own accounting of what’s actually happened. Have we decimated Iran’s nukes? (I thought we’d already done that.) And who is responsible for the attack that killed 160 schoolchildren in Iran?</p><p>We still have no answers to these important questions. When pressed on the school attack, for one, Trump has said everything from Iran was responsible to, <a class="Link" href="https://www.mediaite.com/media/news/cnn-reporter-straight-up-asks-trump-do-you-take-responsibility-for-killing-children-in-school-attack/" target="_blank" >most recently</a>, "I don’t know about it." But an <a class="Link" href="https://www.nytimes.com/2026/03/11/us/politics/iran-school-missile-strike.html" target="_blank" >initial report</a> determined the U.S. was at fault, the result of a targeting mistake.</p><p>As for the nukes, which the White House declared <a class="Link" href="https://www.whitehouse.gov/articles/2025/06/irans-nuclear-facilities-have-been-obliterated-and-suggestions-otherwise-are-fake-news/" target="_blank" >“obliterated”</a> last June, our own intelligence assessment just found that Iran can still access about 60% of its enriched material stored at Esfahan. As the nonpartisan Arms Control Association <a class="Link" href="https://www.armscontrol.org/issue-briefs/2026-03/us-war-iran-new-and-lingering-nuclear-risks" target="_blank" >notes</a>, "Although strikes can set back Iran’s nuclear program and destroy key infrastructure … military force cannot eliminate Tehran’s proliferation risk."</p><p>Trump’s version of events, as is so often the case, isn’t based on facts, but wishcasting, projection, bombast and bluffs.</p><p>And abroad, it isn’t working.</p><p>In France, for example, <a class="Link" href="https://www.lemonde.fr/en/international/article/2026/03/10/trump-team-accused-of-treating-iran-war-as-spectacle_6751310_4.html" target="_blank" >Le Monde</a> derides Trump’s treatment of the war as "spectacle," lambasting his "celebratory tone." It noted his grotesque joke that it’s "more fun" to sink Iranian warships than to seize them, and Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth’s boast that "we are punching [the Iranians] while they’re down, which is exactly how it should be."</p><p>In The Guardian, columnist Rafael Behr <a class="Link" href="https://www.theguardian.com/commentisfree/2026/mar/11/donald-trump-war-iran-ego-trip-economic-damage" target="_blank" >points out</a> the chaos and incompetence of Trump’s war: "Regime change was the plan, but Trump finds it easier to change plans than regimes. He says he has won, but also that he has more winning to do. This is the familiar stage of rhetorical climbdown, indicating dawning awareness that a problem is more complicated than the president initially thought. Complexity resists his whim. It bores him."</p><p>And in Germany’s <a class="Link" href="https://www.bild.de/politik/ausland-und-internationales/profiteure-im-iran-krieg-wem-bombt-trump-die-taschen-voll-69af0a4a234864f234f02e97" target="_blank" >Bild</a>, Europe’s highest-circulating newspaper, the question is pointed: "Whose pockets is Trump filling with bombs?" It declares "the clearest winner in the biggest Middle East conflict in decades is the U.S. arms industry" and Trump’s sons, who are conveniently now in the drone business.</p><p>The world can see through Trump’s charade, but do American voters? Most polls show more voters oppose the war than support it, but by a slim margin.</p><p>That margin will widen with time, most certainly. And then Trump’s slick sales pitch will be less and less effective. Or maybe I’m the one who’s wishcasting.</p><p><i>S.E. Cupp is the host of “S.E. Cupp Unfiltered” on CNN.</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-e70000" name="module-e70000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody">Send letters to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. More about <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/submitting-opinion-and-letters" >how to submit here</a>.</div> </div><p></p>
entries.author: S. E. Cupp

entries.title: Lawsuit challenges Trump's new tariff plan
entries.summary: <p>President Donald Trump's original plan for addressing the purported threat posed by the long-standing U.S. trade deficit, which the Supreme Court <a class="Link" href="https://reason.com/2026/02/20/the-supreme-court-just-struck-down-trumps-emergency-tariffs/" target="_blank" >rejected</a> in February, involved declaring an imaginary emergency to justify tariffs under a statute that does not authorize them. His backup plan, which he <a class="Link" href="https://reason.com/2026/02/20/trump-orders-new-10-percent-global-tariff-after-supreme-courts-rebuke/" target="_blank" >revealed</a> immediately after that decision, avoids the second difficulty but not the first one.</p><p>The <a class="Link" href="https://www.govinfo.gov/content/pkg/COMPS-1079/pdf/COMPS-1079.pdf" target="_blank" >International Emergency Economic Powers Act</a>, or IEEPA, the 1977 law that Trump cited when he announced his sweeping "Liberation Day" tariffs last year, does not mention import taxes and had never been used to impose them. By contrast, Section 122 of the <a class="Link" href="https://www.govinfo.gov/content/pkg/COMPS-10384/pdf/COMPS-10384.pdf" target="_blank" >Trade Act of 1974</a>, the provision Trump cited after his Supreme Court defeat, does authorize tariffs in certain circumstances.</p><p>The problem, according to a <a class="Link" href="https://libertyjusticecenter.org/wp-content/uploads/Burlap-and-Barrel-v.-Trump-Compl.-2026.03.09.pdf" target="_blank" >lawsuit</a> that the Liberty Justice Center filed on Monday in the U.S. Court of International Trade, is that those circumstances "do not currently exist." The center, which spearheaded the legal challenge that defeated Trump's IEEPA tariffs, notes that "Section 122 is limited to international balance-of-payments problems — problems it is economically impossible for the United States to suffer under our current system of floating exchange rates."</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-c90000" name="module-c90000"></a> <div class="RichTextSidebarModule-title">Columnist </div> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody">Columnist</div> </div><p>Section 122 authorizes the president to impose a "temporary import surcharge" to address "fundamental international payments problems" caused by "large and serious United States balance-of-payments deficits." But the "payments problems" that Congress <a class="Link" href="https://x.com/philwmagness/status/2025664144205041695?s=46&t=Sc-Ks1HS4W-tUv-tijhnWg" target="_blank" >had in mind</a> stemmed from a monetary system that no longer exists.</p><p>Under the 1944 Bretton Woods Agreement, 43 countries made their currencies convertible to U.S. dollars at fixed rates, and those dollars could be exchanged for gold held by the U.S. government. That system became problematic by the early 1970s, when President Richard Nixon <a class="Link" href="https://www.cato.org/blog/new-trump-tariffs-are-also-unlawful" target="_blank" >responded</a> to dwindling gold reserves and large, persistent balance-of-payments deficits by suspending the conversion of U.S. currency to gold and imposing temporary tariffs.</p><p>A federal appeals court ultimately <a class="Link" href="https://www.casemine.com/judgement/us/5914c642add7b049347d9f1f" target="_blank" >upheld</a> those tariffs under the Trading With the Enemy Act of 1917. But when Congress considered the Trade Act of 1974, the legality of Nixon's tariffs, which a lower court had deemed unlawful, was uncertain. Section 122 was aimed at constraining the president's authority to deal with "fundamental international payments problems" like those Nixon had faced.</p><p>Section 122 capped the temporary import surcharge at 15% and limited its duration to 150 days, which could be extended only with congressional approval. It prohibited tariffs that discriminate against particular countries or distinguish between product categories without special justification, and it ruled out tariffs "for the purpose of protecting individual domestic industries from import competition."</p><p>Trump's Feb. 20 <a class="Link" href="https://www.whitehouse.gov/presidential-actions/2026/02/imposing-a-temporary-import-surcharge-to-address-fundamental-international-payments-problems/" target="_blank" >proclamation</a> invoking Section 122 complies with the first two restrictions, imposing a surcharge of 10% (which he later <a class="Link" href="https://truthsocial.com/@realDonaldTrump/posts/116109447886304328" target="_blank" >said</a> would be raised to 15% "for a period of 150 days." But the Liberty Justice Center lawsuit argues that Trump's lists of exceptions for specified countries and goods do not include the findings required by Section 122.</p><p>More fundamentally, the center says, the "international balance-of-payments problems" that Trump describes are "no longer economically possible after the end of Bretton Woods." As economist Milton Friedman <a class="Link" href="https://fraser.stlouisfed.org/files/docs/meltzer/fribal67.pdf" target="_blank" >explained</a> back in 1967, "a system of floating exchange rates completely eliminates the balance-of-payments problem," meaning "there cannot be a deficit or a surplus threatening an exchange crisis."</p><p>A look at the <a class="Link" href="https://www.everycrsreport.com/files/20030430_RL31220_72d20f484df9920817eeb4ffa7dcc86d7319f3fb.pdf" target="_blank" >balance of payments</a>, which includes all financial transfers between the United States and other countries, confirms that point. The <a class="Link" href="https://www.stlouisfed.org/publications/page-one-economics/2025/oct/what-is-the-balance-of-payments" target="_blank" >calculation</a> includes the "current account," which consists mainly of imports and exports, but it also includes the financial and capital accounts, which consist mainly of borrowing (such as U.S. bonds) and foreign investment in the United States.</p><p>When imports exceed exports, the difference is balanced by inflows of loans, capital and other transfers. That is why, as the government's lawyers <a class="Link" href="https://reason.com/2026/02/23/trumps-rationale-for-his-new-tariffs-contradicts-the-position-he-took-before-his-supreme-court-defeat/" target="_blank" >conceded</a> during the litigation over Trump's IEEPA tariffs, a trade deficit is "conceptually distinct" from a balance-of-payments deficit.</p><p>Trump has now <a class="Link" href="https://reason.com/2026/02/23/trumps-new-tariffs-are-probably-illegal-too/" target="_blank" >changed his tune</a> and his terminology. But it <a class="Link" href="https://realeconomy.rsmus.com/are-the-new-tariffs-justified-under-section-122-no/" target="_blank" >seems doubtful</a> that his word games will <a class="Link" href="https://reason.com/2026/03/05/lawsuit-trumps-newest-tariffs-are-an-exercise-of-completely-unrestrained-executive-power/" target="_blank" >pass judicial muster</a>.</p><p><i>Jacob Sullum is a senior editor at Reason magazine.</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-7d0000" name="module-7d0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody">Send letters to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. More about <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/submitting-opinion-and-letters" >how to submit here</a>.</div> </div><p></p>
entries.author: Jacob Sullum

entries.title: Is there life after divorce? Amy Landecker gives an enthusiastic ‘yes’ in her new rom-com
entries.summary: <p>The new movie “For Worse” feels like a throwback to the golden age of romantic comedies. </p><p>Chicago native Amy Landecker wrote and directed the film about a woman named Lauren who has just gone through a divorce. Lauren gets invited to a younger friend’s wedding, where, despite being sober, she ends up acting more like a drunken 20-something bridesmaid.</p><p>It’s a classic storyline: Lauren acts like a mess in public, hits rock bottom and eventually makes it through to the other side, a little wiser and better off than where she began. The film — which Landecker says she made in under two weeks, with less than $500,000 — draws heavily from her own experience with divorce.</p><p>She has called divorce the worst period of her life. But in her sweet 90-minute watch, it has been infused with charm and humor.</p><p>“In my real life, I have a really lovely happy ending, and I wanted to bring in the hope of coming out the other side of that situation, whether it's divorce or a breakup,” said Landecker, 56.</p><p>Part of her own happy ending includes meeting her husband, Bradley Whitford (“West Wing,” “The Diplomat”), who also stars in “For Worse.”</p><p>As Landecker prepared for the opening of “For Worse,” she spoke with WBEZ’s Courtney Kueppers about the filmmaking process, her love for the genre and what comes next. The film is now in theaters and will be available for at-home rentals starting April 3. This interview was lightly edited for length and clarity.</p><p><b>You wrote, directed and starred in this movie. When did that process begin?</b></p><p>When I was probably still just separated, I got invited to a wedding of a much-younger friend of mine, and I went with a group of younger people, and I had this very fish-out-of-water experience. Instead of practicing some form of self-care and going to bed as the evening devolved into wedding madness, I sort of joined in. It was one of those kinds of nights where you're like, “Why am I awake? Like, I'm a mom. I should be in bed.” But the experience was so hysterical that almost immediately, I started thinking about how it would make a good movie.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/54b5933/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3500x1964+0+182/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F24%2Fac%2F7218882f49a4bb39c1a2d6f8e1df%2Fnewromcom-260311-009-2.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8e39c8a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3500x1964+0+182/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F24%2Fac%2F7218882f49a4bb39c1a2d6f8e1df%2Fnewromcom-260311-009-2.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Landecker stars alongside her real-life husband, Bradley Whitford. It’s the first time the two have appeared together on screen.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Barry Brecheisen/For the Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p><b>There is so much of your own story in this script. Is there any sort of healing in making it into a film and telling this story in a way that is very real, but also has a good amount of humor?</b></p><p>Oh, for sure. I mean, humor is pain plus time. I feel like now, as someone who's come out the other side so well, it is my duty to share with people that they're going to be OK.</p><p>Especially for women who are older, it's like, “Oh, well, you can't. You can’t go dating again in your 40s or your 50s, or you're not going to get a job, or you're not going to have a new life.” And I've really been able to have a different experience than that. My first real role on camera that sort of put me on any map at all was when I was 40. I directed my first film at 55.</p><p>It takes time, and you fall apart, and you act like an idiot. You think you're going to die. But then, time heals. That's what I'm representing here.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-6b0000" name="image-6b0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275"
