alt="ForWorse_WeddingCakeAmy (1).jpg" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4df3822/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3840x2155+0+2/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F6e%2Fef%2F368524c34becaab364cf1780fe2f%2Fforworse-weddingcakeamy-1.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0bf64d9/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3840x2155+0+2/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F6e%2Fef%2F368524c34becaab364cf1780fe2f%2Fforworse-weddingcakeamy-1.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4df3822/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3840x2155+0+2/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F6e%2Fef%2F368524c34becaab364cf1780fe2f%2Fforworse-weddingcakeamy-1.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>On screen, Landecker’s character, Lauren, has just gone through a divorce when she gets invited to a much-younger friend’s wedding and ends up making a fool of herself in public. Landecker said it’s a story about falling apart and coming out the other side. It is inspired heavily by her own lived experience.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Courtesy of “For Worse”</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p><b>This film feels like sort of a throwback to the glory days of romantic comedies. Is that something you were aiming for?</b></p><p>I'm a huge rom-com fan, like, just huge. I'm referencing “My Best Friend's Wedding” a lot. That was a Chicago movie, and a lot of Chicago actors were in that movie, including Paul Adelstein, who plays my ex-husband in my movie. And it really struck me because it was both a rom-com and also sort of serious, like she was having a legitimately hard time, and she was kind of being a legitimate jerk. And so I thought that felt tonally like what I was doing.</p><p>There's something very satisfying about that formula and the threat that it's not going to work out and then it does. It's comfy, cozy and inspiring, and that's why I like them so much.</p><p><b>Your husband, Bradley Whitford, stars in this film with you and co-produced it. What was it like acting across from him?</b></p><p>What was really wild is, on the second day of shooting, we do the biggest scene that we have together, and I was sort of like, “OK, here we’re going to find out whether we have chemistry.” And it was like butter. It was so easy. It was one of the most beautiful scenes in terms of tone and clarity and connection. And I felt so much relief because I watched the movie, and I feel like I'm watching two other people. I'm into their story and other people seem to feel the same way.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-010000" name="image-010000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ce0687d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6048x3394+0+315/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F48%2Fcc%2Faef022c042e6bb2f5564eb79499b%2Fforworse-20240218-kp-1518.jpeg class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>“In my real life, I have a really lovely happy ending, and I wanted to bring in the hope of coming out the other side of that situation, whether it’s divorce or a breakup,” said Landecker.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Courtesy of “For Worse”</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p><b>You insisted on a theatrical release for this film before streaming. Why was that important to you?</b></p><p>Most of the rom-coms that I'm seeing are really straight to streamer, but I was really hopeful to get a theatrical release, because I went to a bunch of festivals with this movie, and it is so much fun in a group. It is uproarious. There's literally, these audible responses to stuff that you just wouldn't normally get at home on your couch.</p><p>It's a very different thing to laugh with other people where you're all identifying with each other, and I just think that's very special. So I'm hoping people will get their girlfriends together or take their partner and go see this little film we made.</p><p><i>Courtney Kueppers is an arts and culture reporter at WBEZ. </i></p>
entries.author: Courtney Kueppers | WBEZ

entries.title: Hubbard Inn security guard died of 'traumatic arrest' after being pushed down stairs, records show
entries.summary: <p>A security guard died of “traumatic arrest” after he was allegedly pushed down the stairs of a River North nightclub while breaking up a fight earlier this week, the Chicago Sun-Times has learned.</p><p>Bryan Christopher Watson was able to get up after <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/09/man-trying-to-intervene-in-fight-fatally-pushed-down-flight-of-stairs-in-river-north" target="_blank" >falling down the stairs</a> early Monday at Hubbard Inn and “possibly” hitting his head, according to police reports. Afterward, he walked into another room, grabbed his chest and collapsed.</p><p>Paramedics treated Watson at the club for up to 40 minutes before taking him to Northwestern Memorial Hospital, the reports show. Police said a doctor determined he died of “traumatic arrest,” though the Cook County medical examiner’s office hasn’t ruled on the cause and manner of his death. Traumatic arrest occurs when the heart stops due to penetrating or blunt trauma. <b> </b></p><p>Responding officers arrested the couple involved in the fight, including the 32-year-old man who’d allegedly pushed Watson, according to the reports. Police sought to charge the man with first-degree murder, but prosecutors declined, citing the need for a toxicology report. The man has since been released from custody.</p><p>The fight broke out about 1:20 a.m. Monday inside the club at 110 W. Hubbard St., one of many businesses owned by lobbyist, lawyer and city contractor Carmen Rossi. Shootings have previously happened in and around other clubs owned by the politically connected entrepreneur.</p><p>A spokesperson for Hubbard Inn said the bar would temporarily close this week to allow staffers time to process Watson’s death. The club is expected to reopen this weekend<i>, </i>in time for its St. Patrick’s Day events. </p><p>Watson had been a security guard at the bar since August, while also working as a driver for his suburban church and an assistant manager at a dollar store. Stepping in to stop a fight wouldn’t have been out of the ordinary for the 35-year-old Garfield Park resident, according to his family. </p><p>“<a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/11/hubbard-inn-closed-suspect-released-with-no-charges-following-security-guards-death" target="_blank" >He was a peacemaker</a>," his younger sister, Aleshya Brister, told the Sun-Times. "And so for him to leave here while trying to make peace, it’s very heartbreaking."</p><p>His family has launched a <a class="Link" href="https://www.gofundme.com/f/in-loving-memory-of-bryan-bwatts-sizzle-watson?attribution_id=sl:435208d6-05ae-4da7-a000-1687c0d2f5f8&lang=en_US&ts=1773242620&utm_campaign=fp_sharesheet&utm_content=amp17_ta-amp20_t1&utm_medium=customer&utm_source=instagram_story" target="_blank" >GoFundMe</a> campaign to help pay for funeral services scheduled for next week.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/09/man-trying-to-intervene-in-fight-fatally-pushed-down-flight-of-stairs-in-river-north" target="_blank" >Security guard dies after being pushed down stairs at Hubbard Inn, police say</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/11/hubbard-inn-closed-suspect-released-with-no-charges-following-security-guards-death" target="_blank" >‘He was a peacemaker,’ say relatives of security guard who died breaking up fight at Hubbard Inn</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: Sophie Sherry

entries.title: CPS should work to prevent future attendance drops due to immigration enforcement, community groups say
entries.summary: <p>A coalition of community groups and a parent advocacy organization are urging Chicago Public Schools to take steps now to prevent attendance declines should there be another surge in federal immigration enforcement.</p><p>Among the things they are asking for: More frequent updates about attendance trends and designated safe spaces where students and parents can retreat should they encounter a threat while going to or from school.</p><p>“So much was needed for our families that we were not prepared for,” said Ana Lorenzana of the Northwest Center in Belmont Cragin, who spoke Wednesday during a virtual briefing on <a class="Link" href="https://kidsfirstchicago.org/assets/miscellaneous/2026-K1C-Safe-Passage-report-v5.pdf" target="_blank" ><u>a report that her organization and several others worked to produce</u></a>.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2026/03/10/chicago-immigration-deport-trump-noem-bovino-midway-blitz" target="_blank" ><u>It is unclear whether President Donald Trump</u></a> will ramp up the number of agents in Chicago and employ the kind of aggressive tactics that ravaged immigrant communities with fear this past fall. For now, there are no signs of a spring surge, as these tactics are under scrutiny and top officials, including U.S. Border Patrol Cmdr. Gregory Bovino, are no longer in their positions.</p><p>But if it does happen, the school system needs to be ready, unlike in the fall when the response was, according to the report, “uneven, fragile, and unsustainable.”</p><p>“The good news is that we have time to prepare,”said Daniel Anello, chief executive director of the parent advocacy organization Kids First Chicago, which spearheaded the report. “I think the bad news is we don't have too much time to prepare, given where we are, and we really need to get organized.”</p><p>Kids First Chicago partnered with the <a class="Link" href="https://authenticengagement.info/" target="_blank" ><u>Coalition for Authentic Community Engagement</u></a>, a group that includes the pastors of prominent Black churches and organizations that serve or represent many of the city’s immigrant communities, including Chinese Americans and Middle Eastern refugees.</p><p>CPS officials said they appreciate the report’s focus on collaboration and will evaluate its recommendations.</p><p>“We remain committed to working alongside all partners to strengthen our practices and ensure educators and students can thrive in a secure environment,” a CPS spokesperson wrote in a statement.</p><p>While CPS has regularly stated that overall attendance did not suffer much this fall, the report’s authors reached a conclusion similar to <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/education/2025/12/02/as-immigration-agents-swept-chicago-this-fall-communities-stepped-in-to-get-kids-to-school-safely" target="_blank" ><u>a WBEZ and Sun-Times analysis earlier this school year</u></a>: There were spikes in absenteeism on particular days and weeks.</p><p>Higher-than-usual absenteeism among English Language Learners was a particular problem, the group found. In the seventh week of school, 3,000 more English Language Learners stayed home, compared to previous years, they estimated.</p><p>Marlon Henriquez, principal of Gunsaulus Scholastic Academy, a magnet school in the Southwest Side neighborhood of Brighton Park, said once the federal enforcement activity died down, attendance bounced back at the elementary school.</p><p>“Our families want to come to school,” he said. “That is very clear.”</p><p>But attendance among high school students who are English Language Learners has not fully rebounded, Anello said. He said “there’s more work to be done” to re-engage those students.</p><p>The report lays out several recommendations, including prioritizing getting support to communities where there has been a lot of immigration enforcement and, consequently, high absenteeism.</p><p>In these communities especially, the report calls for CPS to publish weekly attendance reports so issues are spotted more quickly.</p><p>The report’s authors say principals and other top staff should get <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/immigration/2025/09/05/volunteers-on-ice-patrol-in-chicagos-pilsen-neighborhood-looking-for-agents" target="_blank" ><u>rapid response training</u></a> and schools should have an arrival and dismissal team with a designated leader who can make sure a protocol is followed when there are threats of federal agents nearby.</p><p>Kids First and the community groups also want CPS to do more to get students to school. They point out that <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/education/2025/12/02/as-immigration-agents-swept-chicago-this-fall-communities-stepped-in-to-get-kids-to-school-safely" target="_blank" ><u>communities stepped up this fall</u></a>, providing walking school buses, carpools and neighborhood watches. But they say the district needs a more coordinated approach.</p><p>Henriquez said where he lives on the North Side, a lot of residents came out to keep watch over children as they went to and from school. But Gunsaulus is in an area filled with immigrants and residents who were too scared to stand outside and be those eyes and ears.</p><p>“Getting to school and leaving school, that's something that's out of our sphere of control,” he said. “Having individuals present, opening doors, saying good morning, saying goodbye, being a familiar happy face, would make a huge impact in the community of Gunsaulus.”</p><p>The groups say CPS should undo <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/education/2025/08/13/cps-safe-passage" target="_blank" ><u>recent cuts to the Safe Passage program</u></a>, which posts people on corners to watch students go to and from school, and pilot a transportation program that could be used during surges. That could include working with trusted community organizations to shuttle students to school or expanding bus routes, the report says.</p><p>The groups also suggest that school safety committees made up of teachers and other staff be paired with groups of parents who want to be involved.</p><p>Finally, they want CPS to step up its support for <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/education/2026/02/12/no-recess-lockdowns-less-learning-how-immigration-agents-disrupted-cps-schools-this-fall" target="_blank" ><u>students impacted by federal immigration activity</u></a>. They say CPS should expand mental health services and refer students for food and economic support “so temporary fear does not create long-term academic harm.”</p><p>They also say CPS should make sure students have laptops and a reliable internet connection to allow for some remote learning or tutoring if individual students are facing a real safety risk to come to school in person.</p>
entries.author: Sarah Karp | WBEZ

entries.title: A man goes into a voting booth...
entries.summary: <p>Wednesday afternoon I voted, early, folding my partisan cheat sheet, jamming it in my pocket, and strolling out my front door, turning right. Taking another right at the corner, and over to the Northbrook Village Hall, mid-block.</p><p>Easy-peasy. In many nations, citizens can't vote at all, or only have sham elections. In the United States of America, our votes still matter, still carry significance, and can lead to changes in policy and values. We've witnessed that, big time, in the whipsaw of national elections. Bill Clinton. George W. Bush. Barack Obama. Donald Trump. Joe Biden. Donald Trump redux.</p><p>Line up behind whomever you please. But is there anyone who can say there isn't a <i>difference </i>between electing one of these men or the other? (Actually yes, my viva la revolution pals who are so lost in lefty dogma they miss subtle differences, like one president trying to give millions of Americans health insurance, and another trying to pluck them off the street and deport them because their papers aren't in order).</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-780000" name="module-780000"></a> <div class="RichTextSidebarModule-title">Opinion bug</div> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h2>Opinion</h2></div> </div><p>To claim otherwise, to call elections "rigged" based on nothing but hurt feelings —"ooo, I didn't win; somebody must have cheated because I ALWAYS win!" — is to insult the core of our national identity. </p><p>Many do just that. Many Americans don't vote at all. Some 90 million sat out the past presidential election. Thirty-six percent of eligible voters. Thanks guys. Of course, I'm assuming those with better things to do would have saved the day. Maybe if they got off the couch and did their patriotic duty, Trump would have won ever more bigly. My faith in the electorate is at low ebb.</p><p>As for the politicians... I scanned my cheat sheet as I walked over. For a bigshot major metro newspaper columnist such as myself, voting is also personal. I've broken bread with eight different candidates on the ballot, at various points over the years. Quite a lot really. Some have sulked off, stung by something I'd written. I voted for them anyway, even though I don't like them, personally. They still seem like they're doing their jobs. </p><p>"I figure, might as well vote while I still can," I joked to an election judge. It was supposed to be a joke, though it isn't very funny.</p><p>The Brennan Center for Justice, a nonpartisan law and policy organization, has a page called <a class="Link" href="https://www.brennancenter.org/our-work/research-reports/timeline-trump-administrations-efforts-undermine-elections" target="_blank" >“Timeline of the Trump Administration’s Efforts to Undermine Elections.”</a> It begins:</p><p>"In 2025, a new threat to free and fair elections emerged: the federal government."</p><p>"Since day one of his second term, the Trump administration has attempted to rewrite election rules to burden voters and usurp control of election systems, targeted and threatened election officials and others who keep elections free and fair, supported people who undermine election administration, and retreated from the federal government’s role of protecting voters and the electoral process." </p><p>It lists 21 executive actions taken in the past 14 months to make voting harder. And here's the really sad part: the 21 actions are color coded. Black is "Attempting to usurp control of elections." Red is "Threatening people who keep elections free and fair." Magenta is "Supporting people who undermine elections" and blue is "Reorienting government away from voter protection."</p><p>Color coded. To help keep all the attacks against free elections straight. For instance, swearing in a staunch election denier, Pam Bondi, as attorney general, is magenta, "Supporting people who undermine elections" while the Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency firing hundreds of workers dedicated to protecting election fairness and cutting millions of dollars from their efforts is blue, "Reorienting government away from voter protection."</p><p>There are many things to worry about in 2026. A war on in Iran, threatening to spread in dire ways no one can predict, not to mention cratering the world economy. A war on immigrants, media, education, free speech, science, health care — basically every element of a decent society. It might seem narrow-minded to focus on just one aspect of the general calamity.</p><p>But casting a ballot — the simple act of strolling to the Village Hall, presenting my Voter ID, going into a private voting booth and pecking satisfying fat checkmarks on a screen — is such an essential part of the American experience. Without fair elections, how does power shift? By violence and intrigue or, more likely, not at all.</p><p>To me, election integrity is the most important issue facing us. If we can't vote, we can't influence government and it can do whatever it likes, unchecked. We see that in action now. One way to understand our current administration is this: they're acting like they will never leave power. Whether they are correct or not depends on the survival of free and fair elections.</p>
entries.author: Neil Steinberg

entries.title: City Colleges students get big boost
entries.summary: <h3>Good morning, Chicago. ✶</h3><p>🔎 <b>Below</b>: A program supporting City Colleges students pays big dividends in graduation rates and salaries.</p><p> 🗞️ <b>Plus</b>: The 2026 Ravinia Festival headliners are revealed, just how much housing Chicago needs to build and more.</p><p><b>📝 Keeping score</b>: <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wtludky-tlkjhhnv-j/" target="_blank" >Northwestern bounces Indiana</a>, 74-61.</p><p>🕛: An 8-minute read</p><hr><p></p><h3>TODAY’S WEATHER 🌬️</h3><p>Strong href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wtludky-tlkjhhnv-i/" target="_blank" >Program giving academic, financial support helps City Colleges grads earn more, study finds</a></h3><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wtludky-tlkjhhnv-d/" target="_blank" >Mary Norkol</a></h5><p><b>Support works</b>: Community college students who participate in the Chicago-based One Million Degrees program are more likely to earn their degree and see higher wages after they graduate, a study released Wednesday by the University of Chicago Inclusive Economy Lab found. It bolsters the case for wraparound supports, whether embedded within a college or offered by an outside organization.</p><p><b>How it helps</b>: Applicants to One Million Degrees are matched with a program coordinator who can connect them to financial, academic, professional and personal supports. These could include tutoring or help finding child care. The program also provides an annual stipend of $700-$1000 that full-time students can put toward costs like books and transportation, so long as they’re passing their classes and attending required meetings.</p><p><b>Encouraging numbers</b>: Funded largely by donations and grants, One Million Degrees often works with first-generation and low-income community college students. Researchers found 63% of participants earned a degree within eight years, compared with 55% of their peers who didn't get the same support. Seven years after starting the program, participants made an average $14,000 more annually than other students.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/education/2026/03/11/one-million-degrees-program-helps-city-colleges-students-earn-more-uchicago-study-finds" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h3>MUSIC >Labrinth, Lizzo, Ricky Martin, Chance the Rapper to make their Ravinia debuts</a></h3><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wtludky-tlkjhhnv-u/" target="_blank" >Ambar Colón</a></h5><p><b>Fresh headliners</b>: Labrinth, Lizzo, Ricky Martin, Hugh Jackman and Chicago’s own Chance the Rapper will debut at Ravinia Festival this summer, part of a historic season that features the reopening of the park's renovated iconic pavilion.</p><p><b>Masters of their craft</b>: Dozens of legendary performers will return to perform throughout the summer, including Paul Simon (July 17-18), Gladys Knight (Aug. 11), Bonnie Raitt (Aug. 22), Brandi Carlile (Aug. 26) and Jacob Collier (Sept. 23). Season highlights also include Lizzo and the rock guitarist St. Vincent each performing with orchestras, as well as shows from Billy Idol, Tom Jones, Kool & The Gang and many others.</p><p><b>Nine decades young</b>: Ravinia will also celebrate the 90th anniversary of the Chicago Symphony Orchestra’s residency, featuring a rare appearance by future music director Klaus Mäkelä. The study found only 31 affordable homes are available for rent for every 100 extremely low-income renter households in the metro area. In Illinois, the number of available homes is 34.</p><p><b>Income, costs</b>: Extremely low-income households' annual incomes are at or below 30% of the area median income. In Chicago, that’s $35,970 for a family of four and $25,200 for an individual. In most of these, people are working low-wage jobs, are seniors or have a disability, according to the report. About 76% of such households in Chicago are spending more than half of their income on rent.</p><p><b>Solutions?</b>: Much of the recent apartment development in Chicago is luxury or high-end because developers can’t afford to build lower-rate units without government subsidies. target="_blank" >Bruce Goff’s creative architecture is hidden in plain sight around Chicago</a></h3><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wtludky-tlkjhhnv-jl/" target="_blank" >Alison Cuddy | WBEZ</a></h5><p><b>Renaissance man</b>: "Material Worlds," an exhibition at the Art Institute of Chicago through March 29, offers a glimpse into the unusual mind and prolific creativity of the late architect Bruce Goff, including his music, paintings and architectural drawings.</p><p><b>Proving grounds</b>: Goff, who grew up and launched his career as an architect in Oklahoma, spent close to a decade in Chicago, from 1934 to 1942, teaching and developing an independent architectural practice. It was here he began to create residences that to this day overturn preconceived ideas about home design.</p><p><b>Self-guided tours</b>: With a few exceptions, Goff created residences for mainly middle class people living across the Midwest and Texas. Some of the Illinois structures still exist, tucked away in neighborhoods from Uptown to Aurora. They may well be homes you’ve driven past, with little more than a small marker to designate their architectural significance.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/architecture-design/2026/01/08/bruce-goff-architect-homes-chicago-art-institute" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h3>YOUR DAILY QUESTION ☕️</h3><p><b>Daily Q: What kind of museum is missing from Chicago? What should it cover?</b></p><p>Reply to this email (please include your first and last name). We may run your answer in a future newsletter or story.</p><p>Yesterday, we asked you: <u>If you could commission public art in Chicago, what and where would it be?</u></p><p>Here’s some of what you said…</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="BlockQuote" > <blockquote><p>“A massive glass globe with an interior depiction of Earth that spins, i.e. a globe within a glass globe as a depiction of Earth’s fragility. Location: South end of Grant Park.”</p></blockquote> <div class="BlockQuote-attribution">— Valerie E. Dudley</div> </div> </div> </div> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="BlockQuote" > <blockquote><p>“We need a monument to [city planner Daniel] Burnham.”</p></blockquote> <div class="BlockQuote-attribution">— Susan Swann</div> </div> </div> </div> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="BlockQuote" > <blockquote><p>“A park celebrating the work of the late Richard Hunt, along the lines of the abundant display of David Smith’s work at Storm King in upstate New York. Bonus [would be to locate it] adjacent to his massive studio, which hopefully becomes a museum someday.”</p></blockquote> <div class="BlockQuote-attribution">— Darin Latimer</div> </div> </div> </div> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="BlockQuote" > <blockquote><p>“A huge statue of [Donald] Trump with his foot about to step on that horrible-looking thing called the Obama [Presidential] Library.”</p></blockquote> <div class="BlockQuote-attribution">— Tom Carter</div> </div> </div> </div> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="BlockQuote" > <blockquote><p>“World’s largest [Mike] Ditka. Right next to Trump Tower, but 1 inch taller.”</p></blockquote> <div class="BlockQuote-attribution">— Don Anderson</div> </div> </div> </div> <hr><p></p><h3>PICTURE CHICAGO 📸</h3><div entries.title: Chicago River Swim will return for its second year in September, city says
entries.summary: <p>Chicago's open-water swim will return to the Chicago River for its second year on Sept. 20, the City of Chicago announced on Thursday.</p><p>Last September, more than 300 swimmers <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/outdoors/2025/09/21/swim-chicago-river-race-outdoors" >plunged into the Chicago River</a> for the first organized open-water swim in 98 years. Swimmers will return this September to complete one-mile and two-mile courses along the river's main channel in downtown Chicago. </p><p>Proceeds from this years event will support Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS) research and support the Chicago Park District's efforts to expand access to free swim lessons and water safety education across the city. </p><p>"The Chicago River Swim reflects my administration's commitment to restoring and activating the river as a safe and accessible public asset," Chicago Mayor Brandon Johnson said. "This event celebrates decades of environmental progress and demonstrates what is possible when the City partners with community leaders to expand opportunity, promote public health and bring Chicagoans together around our shared waterways." </p><p>The event is organized by the nonprofit A Long Swim, which was founded by marathon swimmer and ALS advocate Doug McConnell. Following the loss of his father to the disease, McConnell and his sister Ellen, who was later diagnosed with ALS, launched the organization to generate awareness. The nonprofit has raised more than $3 million for ALS research at the Ozdinler Lab of Northwestern University's Feinberg School of Medicine. </p><p>"The second annual Chicago River Swim is about building on that momentum," McConnell said. "It is deeply meaningful to see this become a lasting tradition that supports ALS research and helps ensure more young people have access to swim education and water safety."</p><p>The event is slated to take place on Sept, 20 from 6:30 a.m. to 10 a.m.</p><p>Applications will open on March 31, and remain open for four weeks. An eligibility review of applicants will take place to ensure they meet safety and experience requirements, the news release said. The event expects to select 500 swimmers, which will be announced the week of May 4.</p><p>The event will return as the Chicago River records its cleanest levels on record, the news release said. </p><p>To ensure water quality remains safe for swimmers, a layered testing approach with the Metropolitan Water Reclamation District and the University of Illinois Chicago will continue to monitor water quality up to the event. </p><p>"The fact that people can once again safely enter and enjoy the river demonstrates how far we've come in restoring water quality, strengthening habitat, and recognizing the river as a living ecosystem, teeming with life, at the heart of our city," Margaret Frisbie, executive director of Friends of the Chicago River, said. </p>
entries.author: Elleiana Green

entries.title: Wind gusts up to 60 mph could hit Chicago area Friday, forecasters say
entries.summary: <p>Wind gusts up to 60 miles per hour could hit the Chicago area on Friday, forecasters said.</p><p>A high wind watch is in effect for northeastern Illinois and northwestern Indiana from 4 a.m. to 4 p.m. Friday, according to the National Weather Service.</p><p>The high wind will pick up Thursday night, with wind up to 25 mph and gusts up to 40 mph, forecasters say. Gusts may peak at 60 mph Friday afternoon.</p><p>The high winds come after powerful supercell thunderstorms <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/weather/2026/03/11/2-killed-after-storms-bring-at-least-4-twisters-possible-record-setting-hail-in-illinois-and-indiana" target="_blank" >produced tornadoes Tuesday</a> in Kankakee County and parts of Northwest Indiana. Those storms left an elderly Indiana couple dead and caused widespread damage.</p><p>Friday's wind could cause damage to tree limbs, loose furniture, and roofing and could make driving on open roads more difficult. </p><p>"When you're driving, especially on roads more open to the wind, you gotta be careful around trucks because they get pushed a little bit more," said Mark Ratzer, a meteorologist with the National Weather Service's Romeoville office. "Just be cautious and keep your eyes open and be aware of what's going on."</p><p>There's a slight chance of snow showers Saturday, and possible rain and snow on Sunday, forecasters say. The temperature will also drop. Friday night's low could be 32 degrees; Sunday night's low around 20 degrees; and Monday night's low around 10 degrees.</p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Maxx Crosby says 'I'm a Raider' — but for how long?
entries.summary: <p>Defensive end <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/03/11/bears-get-second-chance-at-de-maxx-crosby-and-perhaps-with-lower-asking-price-from-raiders" target="_blank" >Maxx Crosby</a> took to social media late Wednesday night for the first time since his trade from the Raiders to the Ravens was nixed Tuesday.</p><p>"Everything Happens For A Reason," Crosby wrote. "Believe Nothing You Hear & Half Of What You See. I'm A Raider. I'm Back. Run That S---."</p><p>Crosby declared that "I'm a Raider," which is technically true. Whether that means he's staying there, or whether the team will try to trade him again, remains open for interpretation. </p><p>The Raiders haven't said whether he's still on the market. Theoretically, Crosby declaring his commitment to the franchise would help the Raiders try to drive his trade price back up. The Raiders are lacking leverage after the Ravens backed away from sending them two first-round picks for medical reasons. Crosby is recovering from January knee surgery.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">Everything Happens For A Reason. Believe Nothing You Hear & Half Of What You See. Im A Raider. I’m Back. Run That Sh*t. 🦅🏴‍☠️ <a href="https://t.co/8IZXiDYMio">pic.twitter.com/8IZXiDYMio</a></p>— Maxx Crosby (@CrosbyMaxx) <a href="https://twitter.com/CrosbyMaxx/status/2031933673084449048?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">March 12, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>He returned to Las Vegas on Wednesday to continue his rehab. C.J. LaBoy, Crosby's agent, said he is ahead of schedule and on track to return during the offseason program.</p><p>The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >Bears</a> remain in the market for an edge rusher, be it via the trade or draft. The first and second wave of free-agent defensive ends has come and gone — with a hefty price tag. The Bears were once the betting favorite to land Crosby, who'd grown dissatisfied with the Raiders after an end-of-season dustup in which he was held out of the team's final two games while they — successfully, it turned out — chased the No. 1 overall pick.<br></p>
entries.author: Patrick Finley

entries.title: Cierres recientes de cervecerías artesanales en Chicago mantienen en alerta a sus propietarios
entries.summary: <p>Al menos cinco cervecerías de Chicago han cerrado o han anunciado cierres este año, y los expertos de la industria predicen que más están en camino.</p><p>Alarmist Brewing & Taproom en Sauganash cerró permanentemente el 1 de febrero, y poco después, la Flapjack Brewery de Berwyn y <a class="Link" href="https://www.instagram.com/p/DVKeBf7DCNW/?img_index=1" target="_blank" >Casa Humilde</a> de Forest Park apagaron sus grifos. Otras dos cervecerías están cerrando: Whiner Beer Co. en Back of the Yards cerrará el 29 de marzo, e Illuminated Brew Works en Norwood Park cerrará el 28 de junio.</p><p>Una disminución constante en el consumo de alcohol es, en parte, responsable. La tasa de consumo de alcohol en Estados Unidos alcanzó niveles históricamente bajos en 2025, según <a class="Link" href="https://news.gallup.com/poll/693362/drinking-rate-new-low-alcohol-concerns-surge.aspx" target="_blank" >datos de Gallup</a>. Pero la reciente serie de cierres locales se aceleró por el aumento de costos operacionales y de producción, incluidos los aranceles al aluminio y lo que muchos sienten que es un mercado sobresaturado, afirmaron los propietarios.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-a50000" name="module-a50000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>La ola de anuncios ha mantenido en alerta a los cerveceros que sobreviven, y varios dijeron que están considerando desde comida de mejor calidad hasta eventos en vivo para mantenerse a flote en 2026 y más allá. En medio del tumulto, dos de las cervecerías más grandes de la ciudad, Half Acre Beer Co. y Maplewood Brewing & Distillery, anunciaron el martes que <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/arts-and-culture/2026/03/10/chicago-half-acre-maplewood-breweries-beer-merging" target="_blank" >se fusionarán.</a></p><p>“Las cervecerías que han sobrevivido son las que se enorgullecen de la consistencia y de que la gente sepa lo que va a recibir cuando compra esa cerveza”, dijo Trevor Rose-Hamblin, un cervecero de larga trayectoria y cofundador de Old Irving Brewing.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-b29638" name="image-b29638"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1692f1f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F90%2F02%2Fb6ac519f44d99771d1bbf2e2a833%2Fgrid-oldirving-oib.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c6def20/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F90%2F02%2Fb6ac519f44d99771d1bbf2e2a833%2Fgrid-oldirving-oib.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/873ecb1/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F90%2F02%2Fb6ac519f44d99771d1bbf2e2a833%2Fgrid-oldirving-oib.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="El maestro cervecero Trevor Rose-Hamblin de Old Irving Brewing Co." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/873ecb1/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F90%2F02%2Fb6ac519f44d99771d1bbf2e2a833%2Fgrid-oldirving-oib.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/43e49d8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F90%2F02%2Fb6ac519f44d99771d1bbf2e2a833%2Fgrid-oldirving-oib.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/873ecb1/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F90%2F02%2Fb6ac519f44d99771d1bbf2e2a833%2Fgrid-oldirving-oib.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>“Todos más o menos sabemos que la industria de la cerveza o la industria del licor ha tenido una especie de desaceleración”, dijo Trevor Rose-Hamblin, cofundador de Old Irving Brewing, que aparece aquí en 2019. “Tener comida y un lugar donde la gente pueda cenar, así como una ubicación más conveniente, son cosas que tenemos y que realmente ayudan”.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Brian Rich/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Además de noches de juegos y trivia, conciertos y otros esfuerzos concertados para atraer gente, las cervecerías están aumentando sus precios para igualar la inflación y tratando de comercializar a consumidores más jóvenes y preocupados por la salud con productos alternativos, como bebidas infundidas con THC (tetrahidrocannabinol) y el CBD (cannabidiol), ambos compuestos de marihuana.</p><p>A pesar de sus esfuerzos, los cierres de cervecerías en Chicago reflejan lo que está sucediendo en el resto del país. Según informes anuales <a class="Link" href="https://www.brewersassociation.org/association-news/the-2025-year-in-beer/" target="_blank" >de la Asociación de Cerveceros</a>, los cierres de cervecerías en el país superaron a las aperturas en 2024 y 2025, la mayor caída desde 2020, cuando comenzó la pandemia de COVID-19.</p><p>Pero eso representa sólo una disminución neta del 1% en el número de cervecerías en Estados Unidos, dijo Matt Gacoich, economista del personal de la Asociación de Cerveceros.</p><p>“Ciertamente habrá más cierres. También habrá más aperturas”, dijo. “Después de cierto punto, al igual que en cualquier industria, se alcanza el punto de saturación del mercado. Hubo indicios de que la cerveza artesanal estaba llegando a ese punto antes de la pandemia”.</p><p><b>El esquivo mercado sin alcohol </b></p><p>Chicago vio un auge de la cerveza artesanal hace poco más de una década. Desde 2011, el <a class="Link" href="https://www.brewersassociation.org/statistics-and-data/state-craft-beer-stats/" target="_blank" >número de cervecerías en Illinois</a> ha aumentado de 54 a casi 300 en 2024, pero la cantidad existente ha permanecido estancada desde el año de la pandemia, según el informe de la Asociación de Cerveceros.</p><p>“Realmente ha habido una carrera hacia el fondo desde aproximadamente 2020”, dijo Brian Buckman, fundador y cervecero principal de Illuminated Brew Works, que cerrará a finales de junio.</p><p>Retrasado por COVID-19, Illuminated abrió su bar en 2021. Los fundadores firmaron el contrato de arrendamiento dos años antes, dijo. “[Nosotros] seguíamos viendo cómo la industria se contraía”, dijo Buckman.</p><p>Una cosa que los cerveceros intentaron hacer es atraer a los clientes que no beben alcohol. Pero cambiar para satisfacer esa demanda no ha sido tan fácil como parece. La destilación al vacío, uno de los procesos utilizados para eliminar el alcohol de la cerveza, puede requerir equipos, ingredientes, pruebas y tiempo costosos.</p><p>Whiner Beer Co. abrió en 2016 dentro de un incubador de negocios de alimentos llamado The Plant (1400 W. 46th St.) en Back of the Yards. Pero a dos meses de su décimo aniversario, la cervecería cerrará.</p><p>El copropietario Brian Taylor dijo que Whiner ha intentado incursionar en el mercado de bebidas sin alcohol y de bajo ABV (alcohol por volumen) con tés espumosos y bebidas de yerba mate.</p><p>“Definitivamente intentamos cambiar, mientras mantenemos, obviamente, la cerveza como nuestra principal fuente de ingresos”, dijo Taylor. “Aún tenemos buen rendimiento con [esas]… pero nada que mantenga las puertas abiertas”.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-c33f42" name="image-c33f42"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e8ee7a0/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F03%2F4f%2F893be8b44736976e13a8b6df3f4f%2Fbeercrash-260315-2.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/40af54a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F03%2F4f%2F893be8b44736976e13a8b6df3f4f%2Fbeercrash-260315-2.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source
