entries.title: Cubs name Matthew Boyd Opening Day starter as he returns from Team USA
entries.summary: <p>MESA, Ariz. — Left-hander Matthew Boyd crossed the agility field at the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs" target="_blank" ><u>Cubs</u></a>’ spring training complex and was stopped by right-hander Cade Horton, who offered a fist bump and congratulations. Southpaw Justin Steele joined them, clasping hands with Boyd and patting him on the back.</p><p>Back from World Baseball Classic pool play with Team USA, Boyd had just been officially named the Cubs’ Opening Day starter.</p><p>“Opening Day at Wrigley is something that is truly special,” Boyd said Thursday. “I understand how much it means to Chicago fans and how much it means to my own family. So there's no other word except that I'm truly grateful.”</p><p>March 26 against the Nationals at Wrigley Field will be the third opening-day start of Boyd’s career but the first in front of a full house. His other two with the Tigers were in 2020, when fans weren’t allowed in stadiums due to the COVID-19 pandemic, and 2021, when Comerica Park allowed 20% capacity for Opening Day.</p><p>Boyd left Team USA after pool play to continue to stretch out in game action as he prepares for the regular season, a task that would have been difficult within the WBC schedule. But he had a glowing review of the experience.</p><p>“You imagine what it's like to put on that uni, you imagine what it's like to share a clubhouse with those guys, to hear your Star Spangled Banner, and to be in unison with all the guys knowing that that's our country's anthem and we're the best in our country, doing this right now,” Boyd said. “But then you get to experience it, you get to toe it up with those guys. it's cool the bond that was created over these last few weeks”</p><p>In an exhibition game and pool play, Boyd pitched in bulk out of the bullpen behind Pirates starter Paul Skenes. Against the San Francisco Giants, Boyd tossed 2 ⅔ scoreless innings. But facing Team Mexico in Houston on Monday, Boyd allowed three runs, including two solo homers by Jarren Duran, in 2⅓ innings.</p><p>“There's always something to be gleaned, whether it's in Sloan Park, or Daikin Park in March,” Boyd said. “And that's a situation that will prepare me for the season because it's very similar to the season in terms of the pressure and the fans and everything that came with that and being in that part of the game.”</p><p>Having Boyd piggyback Skenes made sense early in the tournament. But as they both continued to build up, and with relievers also in need of playing time, the innings puzzle got trickier. </p><p>The Cubs talked in generalities as they hashed out Boyd’s spring schedule, needing his WBC work to line him up for opening day. But they waited to make it official until they were sure the tournament wouldn’t affect his preparation. Boyd said he had a more definitive conversation about opening day with manager Craig Counsell and pitching coach Tommy Hottovy about a week ago.</p><p>“You earn these things,” Counsell said Thursday. “Matthew has earned this with how he's pitched last year and since coming back from an injury. He had a great season last year, was a very important player for us. And we're excited to give him the ball on opening day.”</p><p>The opening-day nod is more supporting evidence in Boyd’s assertion that his best baseball is still ahead.</p><p>After that 2021 Opening Day start, when Boyd blanked Cleveland for 5 ⅔ innings, much of his season was colored by a nagging flexor tendon injury that eventually required surgery. Less than two years later, he underwent Tommy John surgery.</p><p>Boyd’s 2025 with the Cubs was also his first full season back and healthy. He had a career year, posting a 3.21 ERA in 31 regular-season starts, with his first All-Star selection.</p><p>“I'm thankful that I don't write the script because probably wouldn't have written it this way, but I wouldn't change the thing,” Boyd said. “The ups and downs of it — from the second surgery and everything that came with that and just hoping for another opportunity, and then getting to be here, getting to be with this amazing ball club, with this amazing staff and amazing teammates.</p><p>“We have a chance to do something really special this year.”</p><p></p><bsp-listcarousel entries.title: Bruce Weber rekindles connection with Chicago, enjoys newfound popularity as Big Ten Network analyst
entries.summary: <p>Coming back to Chicago is always special for Bruce Weber, but his visit this week was delayed — by a bird.</p><p>“My plane was hit by a bird,” Weber texted Tuesday morning, though most likely it was the other way around. “Do you have time to talk now?”</p><p>I had requested an interview with Weber, who was coming to town to serve as a studio analyst for Big Ten Network’s coverage of the conference’s men’s basketball tournament at the United Center. His travel delay allowed us to talk sooner. And there was a lot to catch up on.</p><p>Weber, who lives in Naples, Florida, is flooded with memories each time he returns to Chicago, where he enjoyed some of his best moments as a coach. He led Southern Illinois to the Sweet 16 in 2002 after two upsets at the UC. He led Illinois to the national championship in 2005 after a stirring comeback victory in the Elite Eight at Allstate Arena.</p><p>But Weber’s ties to Chicago predate his coaching days. Growing up in Milwaukee, Weber would come down to see the big college basketball doubleheaders at the old Chicago Stadium. He saw UCLA with Lew Alcindor (who later became Kareem Abdul-Jabbar), Notre Dame, DePaul and Marquette.</p><p>As an assistant on Purdue coach Gene Keady’s staff, Weber would meet with legendary Bulls assistant coach Tex Winter. Keady and Winter were connected through Kansas State, and their relationship allowed Weber to sit in on practices for the 1990s Bulls.</p><p>“I remember watching film after a practice with him,” Weber said of Winter, “and I said, ‘Coach, how do you think of all these drills?’ His efficiency of practice was … a basic two-man layup drill, he had it tied in with the triple-post offense. He goes, ‘What do you think I’ve been doing the last 50 years?’ I just laughed. Yeah, I guess you had time to think about it.”</p><p>Weber is recognized every time he visits. Whether he’s at O’Hare or Midway, someone shouts “ ’05 Illini” or “I’m a Saluki.” During his first conference tournament with BTN, Weber was shocked by the attention he got from fans, especially Indiana fans. During 18 seasons at Purdue and nine at Illinois, he didn’t feel much love from the Hoosier faithful.</p><p>“They would come and say, ‘Can we get a picture with you, Coach, your autograph?’ ” Weber said. “I’d be like, ‘Oh, my goodness, you threw things at me and yelled at me.’ ”</p><p>Weber, 69, is finishing his fourth season with BTN. Since stepping down as Kansas State’s coach in 2022, he said he has had offers to coach almost every year. But considering the sacrifices wife Megan made during his 43-year career and the trips to see their grandchildren from their three daughters in St. Louis, Kansas City and Dallas, a job would have to be a perfect fit.</p><p>Besides, Weber is doing great work on TV. He’s still part of the coaching fraternity, which gives him a special perspective and inside access. He talks regularly with several coaches, and Michigan State coach Tom Izzo even has had Weber watch film with him and his staff. Other networks have inquired about Weber, but he has made BTN his home.</p><p>“Other networks, it’s so dog-eat-dog,” Weber said. “They’re trying to … who’s going to get fired, all that stuff. To me, I lived that, and I don’t want to be somebody doing that to somebody else. I know how painful it is. I just want to be positive and help and promote the game.</p><p>“With the Illini fans, especially. [Illini radio voice] Brian Barnhart called me one day and said, ‘Coach, thanks for calming everyone down.’ I think it was a couple years ago when they lost three in a row, and I said, ‘They’ll be OK.’ ”</p><p>Weber thinks the Illini will be better than that in the Big Ten Tournament. During BTN’s coverage Wednesday, Weber predicted the Illini would win it. In our conversation, he said their height and shooting could carry them deep into the NCAA Tournament, as well, though he noted their “kryptonite” is on defense, where a savvy guard could put them in a bind.</p><p>Weber sees Illinois as one of several Big Ten teams with a chance to end the conference’s NCAA title drought, which is now at 25 years.</p><p>“Michigan’s No. 1, but I think we have a much better chance than we’ve had in the last stretch,” Weber said. “You’ve got star power in the league, you’ve got depth, you have good coaching. So I hope it is [the end], at least for the league’s sake.</p><p>“If Indiana can win football, anything can happen.”</p><p>After our call, the weather in the Chicago area turned for the worse, making me wonder if Weber’s arrival would be delayed further. But During NBC Sports’ broadcast of the first-round games Tuesday, I spotted Weber watching from BTN’s set. He had arrived.</p><p>“About four hours late,” he texted.</p><h3>Remote patrol</h3><p>Chicago Sports Network will air the Cubs-White Sox spring-training game from Glendale, Arizona, at 3 p.m. Friday. <b>John Schriffen</b> and <b>Steve Stone</b> will have the call, which also will be available for free on the CHSN app. <b>Len Kasper</b> and <b>Darrin Jackson</b> will call it on ESPN 1000.</p><p>• Prime Video’s “Meal Ticket,” which explores the nearly 50-year history of the McDonald’s All-American high school basketball games, premieres next Thursday. The documentary has several local connections, including its co-directors, Chicago natives <b>Carlton Sabb</b> and <b>Corey Colvin</b>.<br></p>
entries.author: Jeff Agrest

entries.title: Acusan a hombre de lanzar líquido a estudiantes de un estudio de baile folclórico en Chicago
entries.summary: <p>Un hombre enfrenta cargos de delito menor después de que supuestamente amenazó y arrojó un líquido a niños y sus padres al salir de un estudio de baile en el vecindario de Avondale, según la Policía de Chicago.</p><p>Robert Villanueva, de 50 años, enfrenta cinco cargos de agresión de delito menor, dijo la policía en un comunicado.</p><p>Villanueva supuestamente arrojó vasos con líquido y amenazó a varios niños y adultos alrededor de las 10 p.m. del viernes, según la policía.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-c80000" name="module-c80000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>Un video del incidente publicado en las redes sociales muestra a un hombre y una mujer, que no fue acusada, en una escalera de incendios gritando, “Tienen suerte de que ICE no está aquí”. El hombre, aparentemente Villanueva, luego arroja vasos de líquido hacia el grupo, según el video. El grupo estaba saliendo del baile folclórico mexicano.</p><p>La Policía de Chicago informó que las víctimas eran un niño de 13 años, tres niñas —de 13, 14 y 15 años— y tres mujeres —de 43, 41 y 40 años. La policía indicó que había otras víctimas en la escena que no fueron mencionadas en el informe.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div Guerra, fundadora y directora ejecutiva de Ballet Folklórico de Chicago, dijo que las acusaciones de los vecinos de que la escuela de danza estaba organizando fiestas ruidosas eran "perpetuar estereotipos dañinos sobre nuestra comunidad" e intentaban "justificar comportamientos agresivos".</p><p>Guerra pidió a la comunidad que respete el proceso legal mientras se lleva a cabo y dijo que el enfoque del grupo sigue siendo "proteger a nuestros estudiantes, apoyar a nuestras familias y asegurar que nuestra comunidad entienda la verdad sobre quiénes somos y el trabajo positivo que hacemos”.</p><p>"Estamos agradecidos por el abrumador apoyo que hemos recibido de vecinos, líderes comunitarios y simpatizantes de toda Chicago que se han puesto de nuestro lado y han dejado claro que el odio y la violencia no tienen lugar en nuestros vecindarios”, dijo Guerra en un comunicado al Sun-Times el miércoles por la noche.</p><p>El estudio de danza también agradeció al concejal Felix Cardona (31°), a la Comisión de Relaciones Humanas de la ciudad, a la oficina del alcalde Brandon Johnson y a la unidad de delitos de odio del Departamento de Policía de Chicago (CPD) por conectarse con el grupo para discutir el asunto el martes.</p><p>Cardona anunció el arresto de Villanueva en redes sociales el miércoles, e instó a las personas a permitir que el sistema de justicia siga su curso, señalando la privacidad del menor involucrado.</p><p>“Si bien entendemos las fuertes emociones que rodean esta situación, pedimos a los miembros de la comunidad que se abstengan de atacar o acosar al agresor”, dijo Cardona en un comunicado.</p><p>Villanueva está programado para comparecer en la corte el 9 de abril.</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Violet Miller

entries.title: Cubs' Matt Shaw added first base to the long list of positions he's played this spring
entries.summary: <p>MESA, Ariz. — Embroidered up the thumb of <b>Matt Shaw</b>’s glove Wednesday, as he tried his hand at first base, was the name “<b>Carson Kelly</b>.”</p><p>First base wasn’t originally in the plans as Shaw entered <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs" >Cubs</a> camp in a utility role. But when backup first baseman <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs/2026/02/25/cubs-tyler-austin-undergoes-knee-surgery-expected-to-be-out-for-months" ><b>Tyler Austin</b> had a knee operation in late February</a>, Shaw went to find a first baseman’s glove to add to his growing collection.</p><p>“They found one in the back for me,” Shaw said.</p><p>Shaw isn’t expected to be the primary backup option at the position, behind everyday first baseman <b>Michael Busch</b>. Catcher <b>Miguel Amaya</b> has experience at first base. Kelly is expected to use his own first baseman’s glove at some point this spring.</p><p>This is the time, however, for teams to work out their contingency plans and get players applicable experience. Shaw hadn’t ever played first base in a game before Wednesday.</p><p>“It could take a month to get as much action and as many decisions as he had [Wednesday] in the game,” manager <b>Craig Counsell</b> said Thursday morning. “So I thought it was a great day for Matt. Couldn't have gone any better in terms of having to make hard plays. Perfect. We couldn't ask the team to hit it to him better.”</p><p>Shaw knew that game reps were going to be necessary to iron out communication with second baseman <b>Nico Hoerrner</b> and get a feel for how far he should range to his right. Then on Wednesday, the Royals hit two ground balls at that gap.</p><p>“We were laughing about it,” Shaw said. “Two 'tweeners. Two balls already, tough balls, but they were pretty routine for both of us, so that was good.”</p><p>Shaw also had a high chopper hit to him that he misplayed. But, in the vein of Counsell hoping for mistakes to expedite development, Shaw quickly realized he should have dropped back to field the long hop instead of stepping into it like a third baseman.</p><p>“Still trying to make the right read and not necessarily change too much,” he said, “but know if there's kind of an in-between ball, I probably have more time.”</p><p>First base was the fifth position Shaw has played this spring.</p><p>“I respect what he's doing this spring,” Counsell said. “It hasn't been easy, for sure. But I'd rather make it hard right now than go into the season with at least some experience at all these things.”</p><h3>Roster moves</h3><p>The Cubs made their second round of camp cuts on Thursday, reducing the spring training roster to 46 players.</p><p>The Cubs optioned outfielder <b>Justin Dean</b> and left-handed relievers <b>Luke Little</b> and <b>Ryan Rolison</b> to Triple-A. And they returned right-hander <b>Vince Velasquez</b>, outfielder <b>Brett Bateman</b> and catchers <b>Ariel Armas</b> and <b>Casey Opitz</b> to minor-league camp.</p><h3>Amaya returns </h3><p>Amaya and non-roster invitee catcher <b>Christian Bethancourt</b> returned to camp on Thursday after Team Panama was eliminated from the World Baseball Classic in pool play.</p><p>“It was really fun, for sure,” Amaya said. “And to be there for my country and represent it is always going to be an honor.”</p><p></p><bsp-listcarousel class="PageListE" data-module data-column-count=3 entries.title: Former Bears safety Jaquan Brisker heading home to Pittsburgh
entries.summary: <p>Former <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >Bears</a> safety Jaquan Brisker is going home.</p><p>Brisker, who was born in Pittsburgh and attended Lackawanna College and Penn State, agreed to join the Steelers on a one-year deal Thursday.</p><p>Brisker, a former second-round pick of the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/03/12/bears-garrett-bradbury-coby-bryant-seahawks-patriots-super-bowl-dreams-championship-pedigree-ryan-poles-free-agent-nfl" target="_blank" >Bears</a>, wasn’t expected to return to the team this offseason. He played his best season last year — and finished with perhaps the best game of his career in the playoff loss to the Rams — but the team didn’t seem likely to devote serious money to both safety spots. They agreed to sign Seahawks safety Coby Bryant to a three-year, $40 million deal Monday.</p><p>Brisker was a physical force, sometimes to his own detriment. He suffered a concussion on a textbook tackle against the Panthers in 2024 and missed the last 12 games of the season. Last year was the first time he played in all 17 games.</p><p>Brisker finishes his Bears career with seven sacks, four forced fumbles and two fumble recoveries over four years.</p>
entries.author: Patrick Finley

entries.title: Philanthropic fund awards nearly $8 million to South and West side development projects
entries.summary: <p>Nine community organizations working on the South and West sides will receive a total of nearly $8 million from the Neighborhood Capital Fund, the Chicago Community Trust announced Thursday.</p><p>The funding is to help the organizations fill financing gaps for development projects spanning from health care facilities and an outdoor pavilion to work spaces. It will also help some organizations add more amenities to their projects, Christen Wiggins, senior director of community development at the Chicago Community Trust, said in a news release.</p><p>The winning projects include:<br></p><ul id="rte-d4ba6982-1e66-11f1-8bc3-b9299e371ce7"><li>Blue Azul Center, an Auburn Gresham facility that will offer mental health services, educational programming and fitness resources. The organization Think Blue received $1 million.</li><li>Chicago South Side Birth Center, a South Chicago site for midwifery, birthing and reproductive health. The birthing center team received $500,000 toward its project.</li><li>The Firehouse, a Back of the Yards community programming space for activities such as youth STEM programs and indoor farming. Nonprofit Plant Chicago received $290,000.</li><li>IMAN Health Center, a Chicago Lawn facility to expand critical care services in the area. The Inner-City Muslim Action Network received $1.5 million.</li><li>Imani Village Empowerment Zone, a Pullman health care center that will include features such as a renovated gymnasium, food market and teaching kitchen. Community development group Imani Village received $1.5 million.</li><li>Silver Fox Café and Kenwood Oakland Community Organization offices in Grand Boulevard. KOCO received $820,000.</li><li>MAAFA Center for Arts & Activism, a West Garfield Park outdoor pavilion. Nonprofit MAAFA Redemption Project received $750,000.</li><li>Urban Market Exchange, a Woodlawn artist workspace and business center. The group received $700,000 toward their project.</li><li>Yollocalli Fire Station, a Little Village space for the National Museum of Mexican Art’s Yollocalli youth program’s headquarters. The museum received $800,000.</li></ul><p>The Neighborhood Capital Fund launched in 2025 and expects to grant $20 million to eligible organizations through 2027. The fund is supported by a number of organizations such as the Chicago Community Trust, Target Foundation, Chicago DNC 2024 and Schreiber Philanthropy.</p><p>It builds upon a previous fund, We Rise Together, that had similar goals and invested nearly $50 million from 2021 to 2025, including grants for 40 real estate projects, in underinvested neighborhoods, the news release said.</p><p>“Development momentum has been building in a number of underinvested communities, driven by dedicated local organizations and entrepreneurs with strong community visions,” Wiggins said in a news release. “Neighborhood Capital Fund's support is intended to fuel this momentum by helping community-based project leaders complete financing and overcome development hurdles to layer in more much needed opportunities for residents and visitors for the kinds of things all communities want and need—quality healthcare and childcare, shopping and recreation.”</p><p>In order to be eligible for the grants, organizations must have secured at least 75% of their financing; be located within a half-mile radius of “economic assets generating positive economic activity;” have site control; and be within Cook County’s underinvested neighborhoods, which the fund said is primarily on the South or West sides.</p><p>KOCO said its grant money will allow the group to make their pop-up events — which drew 150 to 200 older adults — permanent by building their cafe. It also enables the organization to expand its offices to include a food pantry, library, community rooms and business incubation spaces.</p><p>The project has been in the works since before the COVID-19 pandemic, and expects to open the Silver Fox Cafe early next year.</p><p>Shannon Bennett, executive director of KOCO, said the organization plans to hire youth, seniors and people who may have a criminal history for the development.</p><p>“This particular initiative grew out of the dreams and the visions of our seniors,” Bennett said. “We have a partner that understands how to support the vision of people … who often have to scramble for resources, who often don’t see themselves in gentrifying communities.”</p><p>The Chicago Community Trust plans to open another call for projects to receive grant funding in the fall.</p><p>The group’s work strikes similar chords to former Mayor Lori Lightfoot’s signature Invest South/West initiative, which aimed to spur economic development in historically neglected communities. Mayor Brandon Johnson has continued those efforts through projects such as the Missing Middle Infill Housing initiative.</p>
entries.author: Lizzie Kane

entries.title: Former Bears CB C.J. Gardner-Johnson signing with Bills
entries.summary: <p>Former Bears cornerback C.J. Gardner-Johnson is off to Buffalo.</p><p>He agreed to sign a one-year deal with the Bills on Thursday — and posted a photo on social media of Buffalo quarterback Josh Allen smiling with diamond-studded teeth. The deal is worth up to $6 million.</p><p>Gardner-Johnson performed ably after signing with the Bears in late October to help buoy a banged-up defensive backfield. Gardner-Johnson began the season in Houston before the Texans cut him after three games. He later spent a week on the Eagles’ practice squad.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="zxx" dir="ltr"><a href="https://t.co/8WXSPcJMvx">pic.twitter.com/8WXSPcJMvx</a></p>— C.J. Gardner-Johnson (@CGJXXIII) <a href="https://twitter.com/CGJXXIII/status/2032226084972909033?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">March 12, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>Gardner-Johnson carried a reputation for being feisty — in 2020, after all, he taunted Bears players until they punched him in two separate games — but didn’t make many waves during his 2025 stint. </p><p>Instead, his play with the Bears spoke for him —  he had two interceptions, a forced fumble and three sacks. He played in 10 games, starting seven, at slot cornerback. He started the Bears’ playoff loss to the Rams over a healthy Kyler Gordon.</p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" entries.author: Patrick Finley

entries.title: With Super Bowl dreams, Bears lean on signees with championship experience
entries.summary: <p>It was all Garrett Bradbury could do not to cry during the national anthem. Playing in this year’s Super Bowl was the highlight of his career.</p><p>“You can’t put a price tag on it,” he said.</p><p>The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >Bears</a>, though, did. One of the things that drew them to the Patriots center — and eventually prompted a trade last week to replace the retiring <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/03/03/bears-drew-dalman-expected-to-retire-center-ryan-poles-falcons-stanford-nfl" >Drew Dalman</a> — was the success he had last year with the AFC champion Patriots.</p><p>It wasn’t just that the Patriots made the Super Bowl, either — it was how they did it. Second-year quarterback Drake Maye went from a promising rookie to one of the best quarterbacks in the NFL, finishing second in the league in MVP voting.</p><p>“Caleb [Williams] is the same age,” Bradbury said Thursday. “It’s the same process of, I want to get to know them and they want to get to know me, and if there are ways I can help them.”</p><p>He’ll pick his spots, though. Since the Vikings made Bradbury a first-round pick in 2019, he’s learned when to interject when dealing with his quarterback — be it Kirk Cousins for five years, Sam Darnold for one magical season or Maye last year.</p><p>“The quarterback position is so unique …” he said. “There’s so many people talking to them, there’s so much on their plate that you don’t want to be a guy that just adds to that.”</p><p>When Dalman retired, the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/03/08/bears-transaction-tracker-2026-offseason-free-agency-coming-going-ryan-poles-ben-johnson" target="_blank" >Bears</a> front office met with coach Ben Johnson and offensive line coaches Dan Roushar and Kyle DeVan to canvass the league for a center that fit the team’s outside zone run scheme. Bradbury stood out — like Dalman, he was nimble, if undersized. He was available because the Patriots wanted to move guard Jared Wilson, a third-round pick last year, to center.</p><p>Bradbury’s experience — both with winning and developing a quarterback — will resonate inside Halas Hall. When the Bears reached the playoffs for the first time in five years, many of their best veteran players — from Montez Sweat to DJ Moore, Cole Kmet to Jaylon Johnson — were experiencing their first-ever winning seasons as professions.</p><p>This year, with the Bears wanting to take the next step, requires players who have been there before.</p><p>Championship-level leadership was one of the three common traits of the Bears’ free agent signees, general manager Ryan Poles said, along with speed and passion. The Bears’ priciest offseason free-agent signing, safety Coby Bryant, was on the Seahawks team that beat Bradbury in the Super Bowl.</p><p>Bryant was the lone defensive starter to leave a unit that gave up the fewest points in the NFL last year. Poles said that immediately after signing him, he heard from opposing coaches and front office members “how pissed off Seattle was” that the Bears lured him away. He considers that a good sign</p><p>Bryant remembers telling friends the Bears would beat the Rams and advance to play them in the NFC title game. He watched the game intently.</p><p>“It was a big reason why I came here,” he said. “I’ve seen the winning culture, the head coach and just the coaching staff.”</p><p>If there was a secret to the Super Bowl-winning season, Bryant said, it was in the process.</p><p>"Don't get complacent," he said, "and each week,  just continue to get better.”</p><p>When Bradbury signed with the Patriots last year, they were coming off a 4-13 season. Coach Mike Vrabel, though, proceeded to instill a work ethic in his new team. He senses Johnson is the same way.</p><p>“You have to have an identity in this league as a team, how you play football, how you go about your business,” Bradbury said. “I see that with the Bears.”</p><p>And he knows what a conference champion looks like.</p><p>“You don’t make comparisons, you don’t make predictions, but you show up and work,” Bradbury said. “That’s what I’m looking forward to.”</p><p></p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" data-show-loadmore="true" ><a entries.title: Pritzker tours storm-ravaged Kankakee County, Aroma Park, hopes for federal help
entries.summary: <p>The tornado that tore through Kankakee County this week destroyed at least 30 homes and damaged nearly 500 buildings, Gov. JB Pritzker said Thursday.</p><p>No deaths were reported in Illinois and there were “very few” injuries, Pritzker said during a briefing after surveying the damage in Kankakee and Aroma Park.</p><p>“Much of that was people looking out for each other, but it was also a little bit of luck here and there, as I’ve heard from some people affected by this, but either way, we’re all grateful,” Pritzker said.</p><p>Two deaths were confirmed in Indiana.</p><p>ComEd has restored “99%” of power to the community, with full restoration expected by Thursday night, the governor added.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-8a0000" name="image-8a0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" alt="Gov. JB Pritzker speaks with Patricia Kime outside her destroyed home as local officials survey a tornado-damaged neighborhood near Norman Street and Strasma North Drive in Aroma Park, Ill. on Thursday, March 12, 2026" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8de0f02/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F3a%2Fa5%2F4f9751c749f9b34fb959d8b7006b%2Fpritzker-kankakee-tornadoes-aroma-park-2.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c7e828b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F3a%2Fa5%2F4f9751c749f9b34fb959d8b7006b%2Fpritzker-kankakee-tornadoes-aroma-park-2.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8de0f02/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F3a%2Fa5%2F4f9751c749f9b34fb959d8b7006b%2Fpritzker-kankakee-tornadoes-aroma-park-2.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Gov. JB Pritzker speaks with Patricia Kime outside her destroyed home as local officials survey a tornado-damaged neighborhood on Thursday in Aroma Park, Ill. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Buildings damaged by the storm include schools, Riverside Hospital, the Kankakee County jail and courthouse, and the Shapiro Developmental Center, state officials said. The Shapiro center is a state facility that provides services and support to adults with developmental disabilities.</p><p>Citing preliminary findings, the National Weather Service categorized <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/weather/2026/03/11/2-killed-after-storms-bring-at-least-4-twisters-possible-record-setting-hail-in-illinois-and-indiana" target="_blank" >the tornado</a> as an EF3, on a five-step scale, with EF5 being the most powerful. It touched down around 6:30 p.m. Tuesday just west of Aroma Park and swept east for about 35 miles, reaching a point near Demotte, Indiana.</p><p>A couple in Lake Village, Indiana, died in the storm after the tornado hit there just before 7 p.m. Tuesday. Officials identified them as Edward L Kozlowski, 89, and his wife Arlene Kozlowski, 84.</p><p>The storm also produced potentially record-setting hail in Illinois and Indiana that also caused extensive damage to homes and vehicles.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-e10000" name="image-e10000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5ad45dd/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc8%2Fbd%2Fb46b119b47d39f1c1a0f2da7a7a3%2Fweather-260312-33.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0e78a24/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc8%2Fbd%2Fb46b119b47d39f1c1a0f2da7a7a3%2Fweather-260312-33.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Damage from a tornado that touched in the Kankakee fairground and made its way to the neighborhood in Aroma Park, Wednesday, March 11, 2026.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The Illinois Emergency Management Agency was still assessing damage Thursday throughout Kankakee County, Pritzker said. The governor couldn’t yet put a dollar figure on how much the state could offer for recovery assistance.</p><p>“We’ve got to assess all the damage and figure out how much it will cost to restore not just the individual homes and the damage that’s been done, but very importantly also, the community resources and infrastructure,” Pritzker said.</p><p>The state plans to bring its assessments to the Federal Emergency Management Agency, hoping to bring in federal resources as well.</p><p>“Just looking around, this looks like a circumstance where federal resources will be triggered and should come to this community,” Pritzker said.</p><p>“I have some confidence here because I think it’s pretty obvious that the damage here is devastating,” Pritzker added, referring to <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/illinois-governor/2026/02/09/trump-administration-denial-illinois-appeal-summer-storms-disaster-relief" target="_blank" >the Trump administration’s denial</a> of an appeal from the state of Illinois for disaster relief funds for summer storms last year.</p><p>Added Pritzker: “I think the fact that it’s evident just by pictures, really, or walking around, you can see that this community needs help.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-ee0000" entries.author: Jason Lieser

entries.title: New front opens in City Council battle over video gambling
entries.summary: <p>The decision to lift the Chicago ban on video gambling terminals has divided the City Council and exacerbated tensions between alderpersons and Mayor Brandon Johnson.</p><p>On Thursday, a new front opened in the ongoing battle.</p><p>Over objections from Jason Ervin (28th), one of Johnson’s closest City Council allies, the License Committee rejected attempts by him and five other mayoral allies — Jesse Fuentes (26th), Walter R. Burnett (27th), Rosanna Rodriguez-Sanchez (33rd), Anthony Quezada (35th) and Maria Hadden (49th) — to prohibit video gambling terminals in their wards.</p><p>Ervin tried to defer the local bans, apparently sensing defeat in a committee stacked with members of the opposition group behind an alternative budget that includes video gambling.</p><p>But License Committee Chair Debra Silverstein (50th) would not allow it. Silverstein argued that colleagues Anthony Napolitano (41st) had already asked for a roll call, that Ald. Gilbert Villegas (36th) had already seconded that motion and, therefore, that the vote needed to proceed.</p><p>The ward-by-ward bans were then soundly defeated by successive votes of 16 to 2 and 14 to 3.</p><p>Ervin called the move a new low and a breach of the unwritten rule known as aldermanic prerogative that calls for the City Council to defer to the local alderperson’s wishes on zoning, licensing and most other matters.</p><p>“I have never in my 15 years in the Council [seen] a ward-based matter done in this manner. This is highly unusual,” Ervin said. “ We do have some level of tradition… and I guess if we’re throwing all of that out of the window… I’m asking that you guys hold this in committee. I don’t know what the rush is on moving something that the alder put in, and the alder is asking to be held in committee.”</p><p>Ervin, who took part in the meeting remotely, signed off in disgust after Silverstein overruled him and called for the vote the proceed.</p><p>Napolitano said he insisted on Thursday’s vote to prevent Johnson from blowing a hole in the $16.6 billion, 2026 budget <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2025/12/23/mayor-brandon-johnson-city-council-alternate-budget" target="_blank" >approved by a City Council </a>majority that rejected Johnson’s corporate head tax.</p><p>That alternative budget assumed that Chicago would generate $6.8 million by licensing newly legalized <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2025/09/16/video-gaming-in-chicago-proposal-anthony-beale-brandon-johnson" target="_blank" >video gambling terminals</a> across the city.</p><p>That’s based on the assumption that 80% of the 3,300 eligible establishments with off-premise liquor licenses will apply, but that the Illinois Gaming Board would take six to eight months to grant those licenses.</p><p>“This is an economic increase to our deflated economy in Chicago to bring more revenue into our city especially because, me as a border ward — I’m being destroyed by my surrounding suburbs that already have VGTs actively running,” Napolitano said.</p><p>A few years ago, Napolitano failed to block an affordable housing project in his far Northwest Side ward based on the longstanding tradition of aldermanic prerogative. He he viewed Thursday’s vote as political payback.</p><p>“All [five] of these aldermen are for more government, bigger government and government oversight and less aldermen being able to speak for their wards. But when it works for them, they want to be able to invoke their right for aldermanic prerogative,” he said. “When this happened to me, my line was, `I’m in the seat right now, but tomorrow it’s going to be you.' That’s exactly what happened. Tomorrow is today and it’s them.”</p><p>Ald. Anthony Beale (9th), the prime mover behind video gambling, has accused the mayor of working behind the scenes to repeal the ordinance that is a pivotal part of the alternative budget. </p><p>When Johnson failed to notify the Illinois Gaming Board to start accepting license applications, Beale did it for the mayor, triggering 200 applications. On Thursday, Beale branded the ward-by-ward bans “another effort to undermine” the alternative budget.</p><p>“This was just a maneuver to try and blow a hole in the budget because they don’t have the votes to repeal it,” Beale said. “Why should we allow something that’s totally irresponsible?”</p><p>Fuentes acknowledged that lifting that the Chicago ban on video gambling terminals is a "citywide issue" that demands a citywide repeal. </p><p>But Fuentes argued that ward-by-ward bans are the next best thing and should have been permitted during Thursday's vote.</p><p>"I have a ward that struggles a great deal with gambling addiction and drug addiction. Having accessible gambling terminals around the corner poses serious health concerns for my constituents," Fuentes said. "The fact that they would push this vote just so they could kill it in wards they do not represent is disheartening and a bit disrespectful. I'm super upset that they chose this route. I wouldn't do it to any alderman who's trying to do what's best for their ward."</p>
entries.author: Fran Spielman

entries.title: Broadview 4? Feds drop conspiracy charges against 2 ICE protesters, including Dem who ended campaign
entries.summary: <p>Federal prosecutors on Thursday dropped conspiracy charges they’d filed against two<b> </b>of six people who protested outside a suburban<b> </b>immigration holding facility<b> </b>last fall,<b> </b>including a former Cook County Board candidate who dropped out of Tuesday's primary<b> </b>to fight the charges.</p><p>The group of defendants known as the “Broadview Six” will be down to four assuming U.S. District Judge April Perry grants the motion filed by federal prosecutors. It seeks to drop charges against <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/01/06/cook-county-board-candidate-suspends-campaign-to-fight-indictment" target="_blank" >Catherine “Cat” Sharp</a><b> </b>and musician Joselyn Walsh.</p><p>Sharp said the move "proves what we have always known — that the indictment in this case was flawed from the outset. </p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2025/11/12/kat-abughazaleh-operation-midway-blitz-donald-trump-jb-pritzker-brandon-johnson-other-candidates-face-arraignment-in-broadview-conspiracy-case" >‘We will win': Six Broadview protesters plead not guilty to conspiracy, decry attack on First Amendment</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>"While I am of course elated at this outcome, [U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement's] abuse of immigrants and refugees in our communities continues," she said.</p><p>Walsh said dismissal of the indictment "does not change the disruption it caused in my life for the past six months.</p><p>"It also does not change that I was a victim of ICE violence when they shot my guitar and that many continue to experience violence at the hands of federal agents," she said.</p><p>Assistant U.S. Attorney William Hogan wrote in Thursday's motion<b> </b>that, "as the United States attorney’s office does in every case, the government has continued to evaluate new facts, evidence, and information to ensure that the interests of justice are served.</p><p>“Accordingly, the United States hereby moves to dismiss with prejudice the indictment against Catherine Sharp and Joselyn Walsh, in the interests of justice,” he added.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2026/tracker-federal-prosecutions-chicago-status-trumps-immigration-blitz-ice" >Tracking every known federal prosecution in Chicago tied to Trump’s immigration blitz</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>Still charged are<b> </b>congressional candidate Kat<b> </b>Abughazaleh<b>, </b>Oak Park village trustee Brian Straw,<b> </b>45th Ward Democratic committeeperson Michael Rabbitt and Andre Martin. They are accused of a conspiracy to impede a federal officer.</p><p>Abughazaleh is seeking the Democratic nomination<b> </b>for Illinois’ 9th District congressional seat in Tuesday's primary.</p><p>The news comes after prosecutors said they planned to narrow and review the conspiracy case against the group. It means Sharp and Walsh <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2026/tracker-federal-prosecutions-chicago-status-trumps-immigration-blitz-ice" target="_blank" >will join an ever-growing list</a> of people who have been cleared after being charged with nonimmigration crimes tied to Operation Midway Blitz.</p><p>It is also a significant blow to the last remaining high-profile prosecution tied to the immigration enforcement campaign.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-920000" name="image-920000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e314e26/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7506x4213+0+397/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd1%2Fdd%2Feb804c5e45589cb696ee7f18eceb%2Fabughazaleharraigned-111325-111225-004.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ac299cd/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7506x4213+0+397/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd1%2Fdd%2Feb804c5e45589cb696ee7f18eceb%2Fabughazaleharraigned-111325-111225-004.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/bc5540d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7506x4213+0+397/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd1%2Fdd%2Feb804c5e45589cb696ee7f18eceb%2Fabughazaleharraigned-111325-111225-004.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="ABUGHAZALEHARRAIGNED-111325-111225_004.jpg" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/bc5540d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7506x4213+0+397/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd1%2Fdd%2Feb804c5e45589cb696ee7f18eceb%2Fabughazaleharraigned-111325-111225-004.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/63334f7/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7506x4213+0+397/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd1%2Fdd%2Feb804c5e45589cb696ee7f18eceb%2Fabughazaleharraigned-111325-111225-004.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/bc5540d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7506x4213+0+397/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd1%2Fdd%2Feb804c5e45589cb696ee7f18eceb%2Fabughazaleharraigned-111325-111225-004.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Joselyn Walsh plays a guitar in Federal Plaza after her arraignment on a federal conspiracy charge. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Arthur Maiorella/For the Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Federal prosecutors in Chicago accused 32 known defendants of nonimmigration crimes connected to Midway Blitz. Once Perry grants the new motion pertaining to Sharp and Walsh,<b> </b>19 of the 32 will have been cleared. None of the 32 have been convicted, so far.</p><p>The cleared defendants include <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/02/03/marimar-martinez-shot-border-patrol-chicago-speaks-out-after-good-pretti-deaths-i-am-their-voice" target="_blank" >Marimar Martinez</a>, the woman shot five times by a Border Patrol agent last October on the Southwest Side.<b> </b>Prosecutors dropped the case against her in November.</p><p>A jury also cleared <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/01/22/federal-jury-rejects-bovino-murder-plot-after-called-upon-stand-up-overreaching-government" target="_blank" >Juan Espinoza Martinez</a><b> </b>in January after he’d been accused of offering $10,000 for the murder of U.S. Border Patrol Cmdr. Gregory Bovino. Immigration authorities took Espinoza Martinez<b> </b>into custody after his acquittal and have held him ever since.</p><p>But <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/02/27/chicago-ice-trump-bovino-marimar-martinez-crime-border-patrol-midway-blitz" target="_blank" >federal prosecution still comes at a cost</a>, even when charges are ultimately abandoned. In Sharp’s case, that includes the suspension of her campaign for Cook County Board so she could focus on her legal defense.</p><p>The remaining Broadview conspiracy defendants are set for trial<b> </b>May 26. The charges revolve around events on the morning of Sept. 26 outside the ICE facility in Broadview.</p><p>An indictment alleges that, while an agent drove a vehicle toward the facility, the defendants<b> </b>and others surrounded it. Members of the crowd allegedly banged on the vehicle, pushed against it, scratched it and even etched the word “PIG” into it.</p><p>The crowd allegedly broke a side mirror and a rear windshield wiper and forced the agent “to drive at an extremely slow rate of speed.”</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/02/27/chicago-ice-trump-bovino-marimar-martinez-crime-border-patrol-midway-blitz" >ICE protesters keep beating Trump in Chicago court, but the battles take a toll — ‘It’s about intimidation’</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>Prosecutors recently agreed to strike certain language from the indictment, though. They also acknowledged during a recent hearing that the timeframe of the alleged conspiracy is limited to Sept. 26, that it was “spontaneous” with no evidence of an explicit agreement, and that all potential co-conspirators were present in Broadview at the time.</p><p>Defense attorneys have said a key issue at trial could be the agent’s “decision and motivation to drive his vehicle into a crowd of more than 50 individuals who were peacefully protesting the documented atrocities occurring inside the Broadview facility.”</p><p>They’ve said their clients and others were engaged in a “Jericho Walk” supervised by local police, who helped traffic safely pass through the demonstration.</p><p>Eventually a Ford Expedition driven by an ICE agent turned “directly” into the crowd, according to the defense filings. There is no evidence the ICE agent waited for police assistance but opted instead to “drive directly into where the crowd was gathered,” defense attorneys say.</p>
entries.author: Jon Seidel

entries.title: 2026 White Sox Opening Day roster projection: Who's expected to break camp with the team in two weeks?
entries.summary: <p>SCOTTSDALE, Ariz. — Two weeks from Thursday, the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" >White Sox</a> will have played their first game of the 2026 season. Given the time crunch and catcher <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/03/11/white-sox-catcher-kyle-teel-suffers-grade-2-right-hamstring-strain-in-team-italys-world-baseball-classic-win" >Kyle Teel suffering a Grade 2 hamstring strain</a> in the World Baseball Classic, now seems like the optimal time to project the Opening Day roster.</p><p>The 2026 lineup features more youth and talent than last year's. Only Miguel Vargas, Lenyn Sosa and Korey Lee remain from last year's lineup, and only Vargas is regarded as a regular. The Sox have high ambitions after an improved second half, but they still have a lot to prove and are betting on young players to continue their upward trajectory.</p><h3><b>Starting pitchers (5)</b></h3><p>RHP Shane Smith</p><p>LHP Anthony Kay</p><p>RHP Davis Martin</p><p>RHP Sean Burke</p><p>RHP Erick Fedde</p><p>The true shocker here is Fedde being in the Opening Day rotation after a season away from the Sox. The 2024 season was a miserable slog for the Sox, but not Fedde. That year elicits positive moments for the veteran right-hander. Over 21 starts with the Sox, Fedde posted a 3.11 ERA before being shipped to St. Louis at the trade deadline. In his 10 starts with the Cardinals, he posted a still respectable 3.72 ERA. But his play dipped in 2025 as he posted a 5.22 ERA with the Cardinals and Brewers. The Sox are hoping he can recapture some of his past success back with the organization that brought it out of him.</p><p>Burke, last year’s Opening Day starter, will likely get another crack in the rotation. He has the best stuff on the team after Smith but struggled last season and was demoted to Triple-A Charlotte.</p><h3><b>Relief pitchers (8)</b></h3><h3></h3><p>RHP Seranthony Dominguez</p><p>RHP Grant Taylor</p><p>RHP Jordan Leasure</p><p>LHP Sean Newcomb</p><p>RHP Jedixson Paez</p><p>RHP Mike Vasil</p><p>RHP Jordan Hicks</p><p>LHP Chris Murphy</p><p>After Taylor, Leasure, Vasil, Hicks and Dominguez, the Sox have a lot of options in the bullpen. The nod goes to Paez, a Rule 5 draft pick, because of his youth and the fact that having a role in the bullpen will allow the team to monitor his innings, since he hasn’t pitched above Class A.</p><p>Murphy has had an impressive spring, posting a 2.70 ERA over four games. Getz described Murphy as a Swiss Army knife; he can make spot starts but has primarily been a reliever in his career.</p><h3><b>Catchers (2)</b></h3><p>C Korey Lee</p><p>C Edgar Quero</p><p>Getz said he only wants to carry two catchers this season after having three at the end of last season. But Teel’s injury thrusts Lee into a role as a backup .</p><h3><b>Infield (6)</b></h3><h3></h3><p>1B Munetaka Murakami</p><p>2B Chase Meidroth</p><p>3B Miguel Vargas</p><p>SS Colson Montgomery</p><p>UTL Lenyn Sosa</p><p>The Sox like Sosa's bat, but he has yet to find a defensive home. But after slugging a career-high 22 homers in 2025, the Sox will try Sosa at first base to get his bat in the lineup. Murakami has generated headlines since signing with the Sox. He’s a star in Japan and will look to make a similar impact in the majors.</p><h3><b>Outfield (5)</b></h3><h3></h3><p>LF/DH Andrew Benintendi</p><p>OF Everson Pereira</p><p>OF Austin Hays</p><p>OF Brooks Baldwin</p><p>UTL Luisangel Acuna</p><p>OF Jarred Kelenic</p><p>Because of his Achilles injuries, Benintendi doesn't have the ability to cover as much ground as he used . He'll likely see fewer than the 69 starts he made in left field last season. Baldwin is dealing with right elbow soreness but is of use to the Sox because of his defensive versatility.</p><h3><b>Injured list (1)</b></h3><p>C Kyle Teel</p><p></p><div class="PageListL" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" data-show-loadmore="true" ><a class="AnchorLink" id="list-4a0005" name="list-4a0005"></a><div class="PageList-header" > <div class="PageList-header-title-wrap" > <div class="PageList-header-title"> <a class="" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" target="{borderColor=#dfdfdf, pageNumber=1, inverseColors=false, footerBgColor=#000000, bodyFont=font-3, showListNumbers=false, imagePositionHorizontal=null, rteBodyFont=font-3, headerBottomBorderColor=#111010, subTitle=null, stickyHeader=true, listTitleColor=#000000, tableCellBorderColor=#ccd1d5, relatedListFont=font-1, hideAuthors=true, tertiaryTextColor=#6c6c6c, buttonHoverTextColor=#ffffff, tableFont=font-1, carouselAutoPlay=false, items=[{type=Image, timestamp=1773275715000, _styledTemplate=null, category=[{items=[White Sox]}], authors=[{name=[{items=[Barry M. entries.author: Kyle Williams

entries.title: Why the Blackhawks believe Andrew Mangiapane could be 'good fit' in their roster
entries.summary: <p>SALT LAKE CITY — Taking forward Andrew Mangiapane’s contract from the Oilers helped the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks" target="_blank" >Blackhawks</a> get a first-round draft pick for Jason Dickinson.</p><p>But Mangiapane, 29, wasn’t solely a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2026/03/04/blackhawks-oilers-trade-jason-dickinson-colton-dach-draft-pick" target="_blank" >trade</a> pawn. The Hawks ‘‘dug into’’ him in advance and concluded ‘‘he could be a good fit’’ with their roster, general manager Kyle Davidson said last week.</p><p>‘‘I think we’re getting a very motivated player,’’ Davidson said.</p><p>Mangiapane, now four years removed from his 35-goal season with the Flames in 2021-22 but only two years removed from his most recent 40-point season, provides several traits the Hawks lack as a team.</p><p>‘‘He’s a really direct, north-south player,’’ coach Jeff Blashill said Thursday. ‘‘Sometimes we can get a little bit too east-west. We have some young skill that can play that way at times — that’s a good thing — but also you need a direct element to your game, and he definitely does that.’’</p><p>Mangiapane’s first goal with the Hawks on Monday, in which he drove to the net and had a shot by Ryan Donato bank off his body and in, exemplified his approach. He’s happy to vie for rebounds and loose pucks in hopes of scoring dirty goals.</p><p>‘‘Obviously, that was a lucky one . . . but it’s just about being there and not getting boxed out and fighting for those loose pucks in those areas,’’ Mangiapane said. ‘‘You’ve got to get to the net to score goals, right?’’</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">Andrew Mangiapane has his first goal as a member of the Blackhawks 🥳 <a href="https://t.co/Pd7GJlcVhN">pic.twitter.com/Pd7GJlcVhN</a></p>— Sportsnet (@Sportsnet) <a href="https://twitter.com/Sportsnet/status/2031178242279821642?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">March 10, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>His offensive approach might turn out to complement one or two of the Hawks’ young, skilled forwards who seek space to make plays. It also could work on a checking line with fellow veterans Donato and Ilya Mikheyev, which is where he’s slotted now.</p><p>Almost 42% of Mangiapane’s shots on goal in the last two seasons have come from around the net, compared with only 26% of the Hawks’ shots on goal this season.</p><p></p><p>And even though Tyler Bertuzzi (57% around the net) does the same thing and has proved to be extremely efficient at it, Bertuzzi’s lack of speed (only seven bursts faster than 20 mph) limits whom he can play with effectively. Mangiapane (48 bursts faster than 20 mph) doesn’t have the same weakness.</p><p></p><p>The challenge for the Hawks will be unlocking Mangiapane’s game better than the Oilers did. He said the biggest key to success for him is playing ‘‘with a role and a purpose’’ he can take pride in.</p><p></p><p>‘‘I still believe in myself and feel like I can get back to the way I was playing in those early days [in Calgary],’’ he said.</p><p></p><p>Considering the Hawks just traded their best hitter (Colton Dach), best fighter (Connor Murphy) and best agitator (Nick Foligno), Mangiapane’s scrappiness and willingness to get under opposing players’ skin makes him useful, too.</p><p></p><p>At 5-10, he probably shouldn’t fight often, but he already has incited a bunch of post-whistle scrums and skirmishes with timely shoves and chirps during his first few games. It partly stems from his fiery nature, but it’s also partly strategic.</p><p></p><p>‘‘Sometimes you get heated in a situation, and other times you may be drawing someone in to take a penalty,’’ he said.</p><p></p><p>Said Blashill: ‘‘His feistiness is something that draws you into the fight. That’s an important element in the NHL because [when] you have so many games, sometimes you need someone to draw you in emotionally like that.’’</p><p></p><p>It’s worth noting the Hawks have several forward prospects who have similar attributes to Mangiapane. AJ Spellacy also plays a north-south game with plenty of physicality and toughness; Marek Vanacker also scores most of his goals around the net; and Sacha Boisvert is also scrappy and eager to fight.</p><p></p><p>They all will be pros next season, which should increase competition for ice time. At least for now, however, Mangiapane — who has another year left on his contract with a $3.6 million salary-cap hit — fills a niche.</p><p></p><h3><b>Numbers game</b></h3><p>As of now, the Hawks are slated to five forwards age 30 or older on the roster next season: Mangiapane, Donato (three years left under contract), Bertuzzi (two years left), Teuvo Teravainen (one year left) and Andre Burakovsky (one year left).</p><p>That's probably one more veteran than would be ideal, given the number of young forwards the Hawks have, and that's not even including pending free agent Mikheyev, whom Davidson indicated he would like to re-sign.</p><p>Buyouts of Mangiapane or Burakovsky would be relatively straightforward, but the organization seems to like both of them. It will take time to sort out the puzzle.</p><p>Among the young forwards, Connor Bedard, Frank Nazar, Oliver Moore and Ryan Greene are obviously roster locks, and Anton Frondell and Roman Kantserov seem like near-locks. </p><p>That's six more guys right there, which wouldn't leave many spots for the likes of Landon Slaggert, Nick Lardis, Spellacy, Vanacker and Boisvert to compete for — unless changes are made within the veteran group.</p><p>"The majority of the players — if not all the players — in the room are going to be with us next season and beyond," Blashill said Thursday. "[Because of] that, it doesn't matter what the age says on their birth certificates. All those guys are important that we continue to grow them."</p><h3><b>Get to know Mango</b></h3><p>Blashill has taken to calling Mangiapane "Mango," which is a new nickname for him in his NHL career. He said he does like mangoes (the fruit), so he will take it as a compliment.</p><p>Mangiapane is still in the lengthy process of learning the Hawks' systems, but his move to Chicago with his wife and seven-month-old son A.J. has been relatively seamless. He has a cousin who lives in the city whom he will now get to see more often.</p><p>"It's been a little crazy and hectic, but it's finally starting to settle down and get back to some normalcy," he said.</p><p></p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" data-show-loadmore="true" ><a class="AnchorLink" entries.author: Ben Pope

entries.title: CTU plans to join May 1 ‘no school, no work’ day of action, wants classes canceled
entries.summary: <p>The Chicago Teachers Union intends to launch what amounts to a one-day strike on May 1 and is asking the mayor and school board for their official support so students can miss school to participate.</p><p>On Wednesday the union’s 730-member House of Delegates <a class="Link" href="https://www.ctulocal1.org/posts/mayday-resolution/?link_id=2&can_id=17ebc6e1a278c81ea80307c4c5703485&source=email-ctu-members-vote-to-join-may-1st-no-school-no-work-no-shopping-day-of-civic-action&email_referrer=email_3140500&email_subject=ctu-members-vote-to-join-may-1st-no-school-no-work-no-shopping-day-of-civic-action" target="_blank" ><u>approved a resolution</u></a> to join a national movement <a class="Link" href="https://maydaystrong.org/" target="_blank" >calling for a day of “no school, no work, no shopping</a>.” Participants plan to call for higher taxes on the wealthy, better-funded schools, protections for immigrants and other reforms.</p><p>Union leaders cited several reasons for participating, including to “demand ICE out of our cities,” referring to the U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement agency, and “tax the rich to support our schools and vital services,” according to the resolution. It also says public education is facing attacks from “MAGA politicians” who support the policies of President Donald Trump and “corporate interests.”</p><p>That day, the union wants members and students to take part in “age-appropriate” civic education, labor history programming, voter registration and know-your-rights training, as well as rallies and marches.</p><p>“Teaching our students what civic action looks like requires more than textbooks when the President sends federal agents to occupy our cities and the Governor chooses to continue giving tax breaks to billionaires instead of giving our students the school day they deserve,” CTU Vice President Jackson Potter said in a statement.</p><p>The union wants Mayor Brandon Johnson and the school board to make May 1 “an official day of action,” saying the mayor can draw <a class="Link" href="https://www.ilga.gov/Documents/legislation/ilcs/documents/010500050K26-1.htm" target="_blank" ><u>upon a state law</u></a> that allows middle and high school students one excused absence per year to attend a “civic event.”</p><p>CTU encouraged its members to <a class="Link" href="https://actionnetwork.org/petitions/may-day-civic-action-day/?link_id=2&can_id=17ebc6e1a278c81ea80307c4c5703485&source=email-may-1st-no-work-no-school-no-shopping-teletownhall-monday-at-6-pm-for-more-2&email_referrer=email_3140628&email_subject=may-1st-no-work-no-school-no-shopping&&" target="_blank" ><u>sign a petition calling for the mayor</u></a> to take action.</p><p>In a statement, Johnson said he would “work with our partners at CPS and in our school communities to find solutions which ensure there is no loss of instruction time.”</p><p>But he indicated support for the broader movement, saying the effort to organize on May 1 “offers working people across the country the opportunity to make their voice heard through direct action.”</p><p>Chicago Public Schools didn’t immediately respond to a request for comment.</p><p>Kevin Moore, a CTU member and social studies teacher at George Washington High School, said it's important for the union to participate in the action because “public education is under attack” and “we shouldn't stand for that.”</p><p>Moore added that though students may not be in school that day, they will still learn something.</p><p>“We’re teaching our students how to use their voice,” Moore said. “We're teaching our students that they have a very powerful role in defending democracy and that they have a role in building the country that we need, in the future they deserve.”</p><p>The CTU has engaged in similar one-day actions before. <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/chicago/2016/04/01/chicago-teachers-stage-rare-1-day-strike" target="_blank" ><u>In 2016, teachers staged a rare one-day strike</u></a> in support of higher wages and more state funding. That action led CPS to cancel school for the day and set up contingency sites where parents who needed to work could drop off their children. <a class="Link" href="https://www.chicagoreporter.com/liveblog-april-1-ctu-strike/" target="_blank" ><u>CPS filed a charge</u></a> with the Illinois Educational Labor Relations Board alleging the strike was illegal.</p><p>Staging actions on International Workers’ Day, also known as May Day, has a long history. The holiday’s origins date back to Chicago’s Haymarket Affair of 1886, <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/reset-with-sasha-ann-simons/2023/05/01/may-day-marks-chicagos-history-of-fighting-for-workers-rights" target="_blank" ><u>a workers’ rights strike</u></a> where several demonstrators, police and civilians died. In the mid-2000s, hundreds of thousands of people participated in <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/2008/04/30/may-day-leaders-predict-strong-march" target="_blank" ><u>May Day marches to support immigrant rights.</u></a></p><p>On May 1 last year, the CTU participated in rallies and marches, too. Though it was a regular school day for students, the union said about <a class="Link" href="https://www.ctulocal1.org/posts/may-day-2025-history-made-lets-keep-it-going/" target="_blank" ><u>500 students participated in the march.</u></a></p>
entries.author: Emmanuel Camarillo

entries.title: The Triibe staff pushes for union, says journalist fired over organizing efforts
entries.summary: <p>The union One Triibe United said the recent firing of a journalist at the Chicago-based Black news outlet The Triibe was retaliation for its labor organizing efforts.</p><p>Corli Jay, The Triibe’s former community investment reporter, was fired Monday. Another newsroom employee was laid off in February, marking the second termination at The Triibe’s small newsroom in recent weeks.</p><p>One Triibe United asked for voluntary recognition of its union and Jay’s immediate reinstatement, according to a Wednesday <a class="Link" href="https://www.instagram.com/p/DVuZA4Sjdnx/?igsh=ajlpb3A0dmphdnQ3" target="_blank" >Instagram</a> post from the group. The Triibe’s leadership stated Jay “had broken nonexistent company ethics rules,” according to the social media post.</p><p>One Triibe United also created a <a class="Link" href="https://actionnetwork.org/letters/were-one-triibe-united-help-us-get-recognized?source=direct_link&" target="_blank" >petition</a> calling for the newsroom’s leadership to recognize the union. It had nearly 850 signatures as of Thursday.</p><p>The Triibe publisher Morgan Elise Johnson said Jay was terminated for crossing journalism ethics lines, not for union organizing.</p><p>Johnson said leadership will consider voluntarily recognizing the union.</p><p>“That decision will come when we are able to deliver it from a healthy headspace,” she said in an email.</p><p>But One Triibe United said in a news release this week that firing Jay was “intended to send a chilling message: that, though Triibe leadership claims to support ‘workers’ rights to unionize,’ they don’t actually mean it.”</p><p>The union is scheduled to vote Mar. 25, in an election overseen by the National Labor Relations Board. It seeks to join the Chicago News Guild.</p><p>One Triibe United will file an unfair labor practice charge with the NLRB about Jay’s termination.</p><p>The Triibe’s website lists five team members, including Jay, Johnson and Editor-in-Chief Tiffany Walden. </p><p>In an <a class="Link" href="https://www.instagram.com/p/DVu3jEujfDs/?img_index=1" target="_blank" >Instagram post</a> this week, Johnson said Jay requested press credentials for herself and a non-Triibe employee without management approval. According to Johnson’s post, Jay was dishonest about the intent of the press credential and created “reputational harm” for The Triibe, among other issues. “We will not tolerate our brand being misrepresented,” Johnson said in the post. </p><p>In February, a majority of eligible staff signed cards and a mission statement to support forming a union, according to One Triibe United. A February <a class="Link" href="https://www.nlrb.gov/case/13-RC-381808" target="_blank" >filing</a> with the NLRB indicated four employees in the union. </p><p>Three people signed One Triibe United’s mission statement, including Jay, systemic racism reporter Tonia Hill and former art director Ashley Lane, who was laid off in February.</p><p>The union aims to “build an environment grounded in equity, honesty and respect, ensuring our organization’s values reflect the accountability and justice that we seek daily for the communities we serve,” according to its mission statement.</p><p>Johnson and Walden launched The Triibe in February 2017 to focus on the experiences and stories of Black communities.</p>
entries.author: Amy Yee

entries.title: Bears GM Ryan Poles 'checked in' on trade for Raiders DE Maxx Crosby, but cost too high
entries.summary: <p>The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >Bears</a> were in on the Maxx Crosby trade talks — who wouldn’t be? — with the Raiders, but only to a point. As general manager Ryan Poles weighed the effect of adding a game-changing pass rusher against the divot it would put in the team’s draft capital and salary-cap situation.</p><p>When discussing the top defensive ends available this offseason, which obviously included Crosby and free agent Trey Hendrickson, Poles said Thursday he preferred to preserve future flexibility.</p><p>Specifically on Crosby, he confirmed that he inquired, but didn’t like the price. The Raiders were asking for two first-round picks, which they got from the Ravens last week before the trade fell through Tuesday.</p><p>“We’re always going to monitor every situation,” Poles said. “Sometimes it fits and it works with what you’ve got going on in terms of resources; sometimes it doesn’t. We were involved. We checked into it. We looked to see if it made sense, had some dialogue, but I’ll just leave it at that.”</p><p>In addition to whatever the Bears would’ve sent to the Raiders, they would’ve had to accommodate Crosby’s contract. He has $121.1 million coming over the next four seasons.</p><p>As it stood Thursday, OverTheCap had the Bears at $6.8 million in cap space after finalizing their acquisitions of safety Coby Bryant, linebacker Devin Bush, center Garrett Bradbury and others. They could restructure some contracts and even unload at least one veteran relatively easily, but Poles expressed aversion to that last month and has pointed out multiple mistakes the Bears made along those lines before his arrival.</p><p>So the Bears showed restraint as the Crosby market heated up and stepped aside as Hendrickson went to the Ravens for $112 million over four years, Jaelen Phillips got a four-year, $120 million contract from the Panthers and Odafe Oweh landed a four-year, $100 million deal with the Commanders.</p><p>“Just constantly looking at short term, long term,” Poles said of his rationale for avoiding those bidding wars. “What’s going to help this football team now? But also, being conscious of what’s going to happen down the road.</p><p>“We want to sustain success. In order to do that, we have to be very calculated with the moves we make. When we were looking into different situations, we took that into account. We felt like sticking with our plan going into free agency was the best thing for us.”</p><p>When asked if it was more an issue of protecting draft resources or spending ability, he said, “All of it.”</p><p>Without a splashy acquisition like Crosby or Hendrickson, the Bears will have to rely on a combination of players they already have and prospects in the draft.</p><p>Their best asset in the pass rush is defensive end Montez Sweat, who has been up and down in his Bears tenure but had 10 sacks last season. Defensive tackle Gervon Dexter was next with six, and defensive end Austin Booker had 4 1/2 as the team finished with the seventh-fewest sacks in the NFL.</p><p>The Bears have had the second-fewest sacks in the league during Poles’ four seasons, and he hoped he’d solved that problem long term by signing former Colts defensive end Dayo Odeyingbo a year ago to pair with Sweat.</p><p>Odeyingbo, though, had just one sack in eight games before tearing his Achilles. The Bears also drafted Shemar Turner in the second round and liked his progress at defensive end, but he tore his ACL midseason.</p><p>Their top picks in the draft are Nos. 25, 57 and 60 overall, so their options are limited. They also must consider pressing needs at offensive tackle, defensive tackle and possibly cornerback, safety and wide receiver.</p><p>The top three pass rushers were gone by the 25th pick last year, and the top four were off the board by then in the previous two drafts.</p><p>The other option for the Bears is to shop among veterans who likely would be available on a pricy one-year deal. Cameron Jordan, Joey Bosa and Haason Reddick were unsigned Thursday.</p><p>Poles was definitively speaking in the past tense about looking into Crosby, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/03/11/bears-get-second-chance-at-de-maxx-crosby-and-perhaps-with-lower-asking-price-from-raiders" >though that could change if the Raiders’ demands drop</a>. They might have to consider taking less if they’re still intent on unloading him soon.</p><p>For his part, Crosby expressed enthusiasm for staying with the organization in a post on X and appeared to push back against the Ravens’ concerns about his knee two months after undergoing surgery on it.</p><p>“Everything happens for a reason... I’m back,” he wrote.</p><p>The market for him could surge again as it gets closer to the start of the season, so this is the time for an opportunist to swoop in. If Crosby looks like he’s back to normal and teams start to become a little more desperate, someone might give the Raiders two first-rounders in August.</p><p>Poles, however, didn’t sound ready to take plunge on that.</p><p></p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" data-show-loadmore="true" ><a
